>  
> **Key Implications:**  
> 1. **No Extra Work**: The WBS cannot include work that is *outside* the project scope.  
> 2. **No Missing Work**: Every piece of in-scope work must appear in the WBS exactly once.  
> 3. **Children = Parent**: At any level, the sum of the child elements must equal **100% of the work** of the parent element.  
> 4. **Includes PM Effort**: Project management activities (planning, controlling, reporting) must also be a WBS element.

## 2.4 Steps to Create a WBS (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. **Identify the Final Deliverable(s)** — Start with the project's main output(s).
2. **Define Major Deliverables (Level 1)** — Break the project into its principal sub-deliverables.
3. **Decompose Each Deliverable** — Continue breaking down until work-package level is reached.
4. **Assign WBS Codes** — Apply a unique Code of Accounts to every element.
5. **Verify the 100% Rule** — Cross-check that all scope is captured and nothing extra is included.
6. **Create the WBS Dictionary** — Document the details of every work package.
7. **Obtain Formal Sign-Off** — Stakeholder and sponsor approval of the WBS is mandatory before scheduling begins.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table summarises the key **mathematical and logical relationships** used in WBS-based cost and schedule aggregation. These are the **most frequently tested computations** in KTU ESE questions.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------------|--------------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Cost Aggregation (Bottom-Up Estimation)** | $C_{parent} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{child,i}$ | Parent element cost equals the sum of its child element costs | Currency (₹, $, etc.) |
| **Total Project Cost** | $C_{project} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{WP,j}$ | Project cost is the sum of all work package costs | Currency |
| **Work Package Effort** | $E_{WP} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} (Rate_k \times Hours_k)$ | Effort = sum of (labour rate × hours) across all resources | Person-Hours or ₹ |
| **100% Rule Check (Cost)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{child,i} = C_{parent}$ | Sum of child costs must exactly equal parent cost (within rounding) | Currency |
| **100% Rule Check (Scope Count)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 1_{child,i} = N_{parent}$ | Every parent element contains all and only its child work items | Count |
| **Work Package Size (8/80 Heuristic)** | $8 \le E_{WP} \le 80$ | Work package effort should lie between 8 and 80 person-hours | Person-Hours |
| **Decomposition Level Limit** | $L_{max} = \text{floor}\left(\log_{N}\left(\frac{T}{E_{min}}\right)\right)$ | Maximum decomposition depth, where $N$ = avg. children per node, $T$ = total effort, $E_{min}$ = min work-package effort | Levels |
| **WBS Code Hierarchy** | $Code = X.Y.Z.W$ | Typical 4-level alphanumeric code (Project.Major.Sub.WorkPackage) | Alphanumeric |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industries like **construction (L&T, Tata Projects)**, **software (TCS, Infosys)**, **manufacturing (ISRO, DRDO)**, and **public infrastructure (NHAI, Kochi Metro)**, the WBS is the **backbone of project control**. It enables:
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)** — measuring cost and schedule performance using Control Accounts.
- **Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM / RACI chart)** — assigning accountability at the work-package level.
- **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)** — risks are often mapped to WBS elements.
- **Procurement Planning** — purchase orders are aligned with specific WBS elements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed WBS Construction — Worked Example (House Construction Project)

To make the theory concrete, let us construct a **WBS for a Residential House Construction Project** step by step. This type of construction is a **classic KTU exam question**.

### Step 1: Identify the Final Deliverable
- **Project Name**: Construction of a 2-BHK Residential House
- **Final Deliverable (Level 0)**: Completed, handed-over 2-BHK house

### Step 2: Identify Major Deliverables (Level 1)
Using a **deliverable-oriented** approach, we break the house into major physical components:

- **1.0** Site Preparation & Foundation
- **2.0** Superstructure (Walls, Columns, Slabs)
- **3.0** Roofing
- **4.0** Electrical Works
- **5.0** Plumbing Works
- **6.0** Interior Finishing
- **7.0** Project Management

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Note:** *Project Management (7.0)* is included as a separate Level-1 element to satisfy the **100% Rule**, since planning, supervision, and reporting effort is part of the project's scope.

### Step 3: Decompose Each Major Deliverable (Level 2)
We now decompose one branch in detail. Consider **1.0 Site Preparation & Foundation**:

- **1.1** Land Survey & Soil Testing
- **1.2** Site Clearing & Excavation
- **1.3** Footing Construction
  - **1.3.1** Formwork (Work Package)
  - **1.3.2** Reinforcement Placement (Work Package)
  - **1.3.3** Concrete Pouring & Curing (Work Package)
- **1.4** Plinth Beam
- **1.5** Backfilling

Each of the bolded items (1.3.1, 1.3.2, 1.3.3) is a **Work Package** — small enough to be estimated, scheduled, and controlled by a single foreman/team.

### Step 4: Verify the 100% Rule
We check whether every Level-2 child fully describes its Level-1 parent.

$$
\text{Work under } 1.0 = 1.1 + 1.2 + 1.3 + 1.4 + 1.5
$$

If this equals the total work in "Site Preparation & Foundation", the 100% rule is satisfied for that branch. This check is repeated for every Level-1 element.

## 3.2 Cost Aggregation Derivation

Let us derive the **bottom-up cost aggregation** formula, which is heavily tested.

> **Given:** A WBS element $E_k$ has $n$ child elements $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$, each with a known cost $c_i$ (₹).

> **To Prove:** $C_{E_k} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ (i.e., parent cost equals sum of children costs).

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } C_{E_k} &= \text{Total cost of element } E_k \\
\text{By definition of a work package: } \quad C_{E_k} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \\
\therefore \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i &= C_{E_k} \quad \text{(Proved)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose the work packages under 1.3 (Footing Construction) have the following costs:

- 1.3.1 Formwork: ₹ 50,000
- 1.3.2 Reinforcement: ₹ 1,20,000
- 1.3.3 Concrete Pouring: ₹ 80,000

The cost of **1.3 Footing Construction** is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1.3} &= c_{1.3.1} + c_{1.3.2} + c_{1.3.3} \\
C_{1.3} &= 50{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 80{,}000 \\
C_{1.3} &= 2{,}50{,}000 \ \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Effort Estimation for a Work Package

A work package often requires multiple resources (e.g., masons, helpers, electricians). The total effort cost is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{WP} &= (R_{mason} \times H_{mason}) + (R_{helper} \times H_{helper}) + (R_{elec} \times H_{elec}) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{p} (R_k \times H_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $R_k$ = hourly/daily rate of resource $k$ (in ₹)
- $H_k$ = hours/days worked by resource $k$
- $p$ = total number of resources on the work package

**Numerical Example:** For work package 1.3.3 (Concrete Pouring):

- 2 masons × 8 hours × ₹ 250/hr = ₹ 4,000
- 4 helpers × 8 hours × ₹ 150/hr = ₹ 4,800
- 1 supervisor × 4 hours × ₹ 500/hr = ₹ 2,000
- Concrete material cost = ₹ 30,000

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1.3.3} &= 4{,}000 + 4{,}800 + 2{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}800 \ \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — WBS Element Verification Table

> [!NOTE]  
> The following **analytical matrix** is a KTU-style verification tool. For each WBS element, students may be asked to confirm compliance with the 100% rule and the 8/80 rule.

| **WBS Code** | **WBS Element** | **Type** | **Parent** | **Estimated Effort (Hours)** | **Estimated Cost (₹)** | **100% Rule Met?** | **8/80 Rule Met?** |
|--------------|-----------------|----------|------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1.0 | Site Preparation & Foundation | Level 1 | 0.0 | 320 | 5,00,000 | ✔ | N/A (Parent) |
| 1.1 | Land Survey & Soil Testing | Level 2 | 1.0 | 24 | 15,000 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.2 | Site Clearing & Excavation | Level 2 | 1.0 | 48 | 40,000 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.3 | Footing Construction | Level 2 | 1.0 | 160 | 2,50,000 | ✔ | ✖ (> 80, needs further decomposition) |
| 1.3.1 | Formwork | Work Package (L3) | 1.3 | 56 | 50,000 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.3.2 | Reinforcement | Work Package (L3) | 1.3 | 64 | 1,20,000 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.3.3 | Concrete Pouring & Curing | Work Package (L3) | 1.3 | 40 | 40,800 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.4 | Plinth Beam | Level 2 | 1.0 | 48 | 1,00,000 | ✔ | ✔ |
| 1.5 | Backfilling | Level 2 | 1.0 | 40 | 95,000 | ✔ | ✔ |

**Interpretation of the Matrix:**
- The element **1.3 (160 hours)** violates the **8/80 Rule** because it exceeds 80 hours. Therefore, it must be **further decomposed** into work packages (1.3.1, 1.3.2, 1.3.3), which now individually satisfy the 8/80 rule (40, 56, 64 hours — all within 8–80).
- The **100% Rule check** for element 1.3 confirms: $50{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}800 = 2{,}10{,}800$, which approximates the parent's 2,50,000 (the difference is for non-decomposed contingency/overhead, which must be explicitly accounted for in the WBS dictionary).

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often forget to include **Project Management** as a separate WBS element. This violates the **100% Rule** and typically results in a **2-mark deduction** in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical WBS Tree for the House Construction Project

The following Mermaid diagram visually represents the **hierarchical decomposition** of the WBS we derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["0.0 Residential House Project"]
    A1["1.0 Site Prep and Foundation"]
    A2["2.0 Superstructure"]
    A3["3.0 Roofing"]
    A4["4.0 Electrical Works"]
    A5["5.0 Plumbing Works"]
    A6["6.0 Interior Finishing"]
    A7["7.0 Project Management"]

    B11["1.1 Land Survey and Soil Testing"]
    B12["1.2 Site Clearing and Excavation"]
    B13["1.3 Footing Construction"]
    B14["1.4 Plinth Beam"]
    B15["1.5 Backfilling"]

    C131["1.3.1 Formwork WP"]
    C132["1.3.2 Reinforcement WP"]
    C133["1.3.3 Concrete Pouring WP"]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A0 --> A4
    A0 --> A5
    A0 --> A6
    A0 --> A7

    A1 --> B11
    A1 --> B12
    A1 --> B13
    A1 --> B14
    A1 --> B15

    B13 --> C131
    B13 --> C132
    B13 --> C133
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **root node (0.0)** represents the entire project.
- The **second tier (1.0 to 7.0)** shows the seven Level-1 deliverables.
- The **third tier under 1.0** shows Level-2 sub-deliverables.
- The **fourth tier under 1.3** shows the **Work Packages** (the lowest controllable level).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: WBS Creation Process Flow

This diagram depicts the **sequential process** a project manager follows to develop a WBS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Identify Project Scope and Final Deliverable"]
    P2["Step 2: Identify Level 1 Major Deliverables"]
    P3["Step 3: Decompose Each Deliverable to Work Package Level"]
    P4["Step 4: Assign WBS Codes of Accounts"]
    P5["Step 5: Apply the 100 Percent Rule Verification"]
    P6["Step 6: Apply the 8 by 80 Rule Verification"]
    P7["Step 7: Prepare the WBS Dictionary"]
    P8["Step 8: Obtain Stakeholder Sign Off"]
    P9["WBS Approved and Baselined"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
```

**Interpretation of the Process Flow:**
- The process is **iterative in practice** — Steps 5 and 6 may send the project manager back to Step 3 for further decomposition if a violation is found.
- The **final output** is a **baselined WBS**, which becomes the official reference for schedule, cost, and scope control.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — WBS and Its Downstream Uses

This diagram illustrates how the WBS serves as the **central hub** for all subsequent project management activities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WBS["Work Breakdown Structure WBS"]
    SCHED["Project Schedule and Gantt Chart"]
    COST["Cost Estimation and Budgeting"]
    RAM["Responsibility Assignment Matrix RAM"]
    RBS["Risk Breakdown Structure RBS"]
    EVM["Earned Value Management"]
    PROC["Procurement Planning"]
    REPORT["Progress Reporting and Control"]

    WBS --> SCHED
    WBS --> COST
    WBS --> RAM
    WBS --> RBS
    WBS --> EVM
    WBS --> PROC
    WBS --> REPORT
```

**Engineering Significance:**
- The WBS is **not an isolated document** — it is the **integration point** between scope, schedule, cost, risk, and resource management.
- Any change to the WBS cascades through all downstream systems, which is why WBS **change control** is a critical process in mature project management offices (PMOs).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Work Breakdown Structure. State the 100% Rule. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total scope of work to be executed by the project team to accomplish the project objectives and produce the required deliverables. It subdivides the project into smaller, more manageable components called work packages, each of which can be estimated, scheduled, monitored, and controlled.

**The 100% Rule** states that the WBS must capture **100% of the project scope** — every piece of work defined by the project scope must be included in the WBS, and no extra work outside the scope may appear in it. In other words, at any level, the sum of the child elements must fully represent the work of their parent element, and all work — including project management effort — must be accounted for.

### Question 2: What is a WBS Dictionary? Mention any four contents of a WBS Dictionary. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **WBS Dictionary** is a supporting document that provides detailed information about each element of the WBS. It complements the WBS diagram by describing *what* is to be done, *who* will do it, *when* it must be done, and *how* performance will be measured.

**Four essential contents of a WBS Dictionary:**

1. **WBS Code / Identifier** — Unique code of accounts for the element.
2. **Description of Work** — A statement of the work to be performed.
3. **Responsible Organisation / Person** — The individual or team accountable.
4. **Schedule Milestones and Cost Estimate** — Key dates and budgeted cost.

*(Other valid contents: quality requirements, acceptance criteria, technical references, contractual information, list of billable / non-billable items.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question is internally divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, or alternatively a 14-mark descriptive answer. **Students must answer only ONE of the two choice questions** provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of Work Breakdown Structures. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A software company is developing a **Hospital Management System (HMS)**. Develop a WBS (up to Level 3) for this project, including the Code of Accounts, and verify it against the 100% Rule. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Types of WBS (7 Marks):**

There are three primary types of WBS structures, and organisations often use a **hybrid** combination:

**1. Deliverable-Oriented (Product-Oriented) WBS:**
The decomposition is based on the **physical or functional components of the final product**. For example, a car project may be broken into Engine, Chassis, Body, Electronics.
- *Advantage*: Directly maps to the end product, making verification of completeness easy.
- *Limitation*: Cross-functional activities (testing, project management) are difficult to assign.

**2. Phase-Oriented (Life-Cycle-Oriented) WBS:**
The decomposition follows the **project life cycle** — Initiation, Planning, Execution, Monitoring, Closure.
- *Advantage*: Reflects the chronological flow of work and aligns naturally with stage-gate reviews.
- *Limitation*: A deliverable may appear in multiple phases, causing redundancy.

**3. Responsibility-Oriented (Organisational-Oriented) WBS:**
The decomposition is based on the **organisational unit** responsible for each piece of work.
- *Advantage*: Simplifies assignment of accountability and resource planning.
- *Limitation*: Loses the "deliverable focus" and may fragment scope across departments.

> A **Hybrid WBS** combines these orientations — for example, deliverable-oriented at the top and responsibility-oriented at the bottom.

**(b) WBS for Hospital Management System (7 Marks):**

**Level 0:**
- **0.0** Hospital Management System (HMS) Project

**Level 1 — Major Deliverables:**
- **1.0** Project Management
- **2.0** System Analysis and Design
- **3.0** Module Development
  - **3.1** Patient Registration Module
  - **3.2** Doctor and Appointment Module
  - **3.3** Pharmacy and Inventory Module
  - **3.4** Billing and Insurance Module
- **4.0** Database Design and Implementation
- **5.0** Testing and Quality Assurance
  - **5.1** Unit Testing
  - **5.2** Integration Testing
  - **5.3** User Acceptance Testing
- **6.0** Deployment and Training
- **7.0** Documentation and Support

**100% Rule Verification:** The Level-1 elements (1.0 through 7.0) cover all aspects — management, design, development, database, testing, deployment, and documentation — satisfying 100% Rule for an HMS project.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **8/80 Rule** and the **100% Rule** with suitable examples. Why are these rules important in WBS development? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) for organising a National-Level Technical Fest** in a college. Show the WBS in an **indented outline format** up to Level 3 and identify at least **three work packages**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) 8/80 Rule and 100% Rule (7 Marks):**

**The 8/80 Rule** is a heuristic for **decomposition depth**. It states that a work package should require between **8 hours and 80 hours of effort** to complete. If a work package exceeds 80 hours, it must be further decomposed; if it is less than 8 hours, it has been over-decomposed and is inefficient to manage.
*Example:* If "Footing Construction" is estimated at 160 hours, it must be broken into Formwork (56 hrs), Reinforcement (64 hrs), and Concrete Pouring (40 hrs) — all satisfying 8 ≤ x ≤ 80.

**The 100% Rule** states that the WBS must represent 100% of the project scope and capture all deliverables. The sum of the children at any level must equal the parent, and project management work must also be included.
*Example:* For the HMS project, the sum of (1.0 PM + 2.0 Design + 3.0 Modules + 4.0 Database + 5.0 Testing + 6.0 Deployment + 7.0 Documentation) must represent the total HMS scope.

**Importance:**
- The **8/80 Rule** ensures that work packages are at the right granularity for **estimation, scheduling, and control**.
- The **100% Rule** guarantees **scope completeness** and forms the basis for accurate **cost aggregation and earned value management**.

**(b) WBS for National-Level Technical Fest (7 Marks):**

**Indented Outline Format:**

```
0.0  National-Level Technical Fest
  1.0  Project Management and Coordination
    1.1  Project Planning
    1.2  Budget and Sponsor Management
    1.3  Risk Management
  2.0  Marketing and Publicity
    2.1  Social Media Campaigns
    2.2  Poster and Banner Design
    2.3  Sponsor Outreach
  3.0  Event Management
    3.1  Inauguration Ceremony
      3.1.1  Stage Setup [WORK PACKAGE]
      3.1.2  Guest Invitations and Hospitality [WORK PACKAGE]
    3.2  Technical Events
      3.2.1  Paper Presentation [WORK PACKAGE]
      3.2.2  Project Expo [WORK PACKAGE]
    3.3  Non-Technical Events
  4.0  Logistics
    4.1  Venue Booking
    4.2  Accommodation and Food
  5.0  Finance and Accounts
  6.0  Post-Event Reporting
```

**Identified Work Packages:**
1. **3.1.1 Stage Setup** — concrete, estimable, and assignable to a single team.
2. **3.2.1 Paper Presentation** — a defined technical event with clear scope.
3. **3.1.2 Guest Invitations and Hospitality** — a manageable, stand-alone logistical task.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in WBS Questions (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Project Management element** — Always include a separate WBS element for project management effort; otherwise, the 100% Rule is violated. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing WBS with Project Schedule** — The WBS defines **WHAT** is to be done (deliverables), not **WHEN** (activities/schedule). Mixing them is a common conceptual error. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Decomposing to Activity Level** — The WBS should stop at the **work package** level. Going further into individual activities belongs in the project schedule, not the WBS. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Not assigning WBS Codes** — KTU expects a clear **Code of Accounts (e.g., 1.3.2)** for every element. Omitting codes leads to a **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 5. **Writing a WBS as a flat list** — A WBS is a **hierarchy**, not a checklist. Use indented outline or tree diagram format. Examiners award marks for the **structure**, not just the content.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):**
>
> ✅ **Definition** — Hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total project scope.
>
> ✅ **Purpose** — Foundation for scheduling, costing, risk, resource, and procurement planning.
>
> ✅ **Work Package** — Lowest level of WBS; estimable, schedulable, controllable; effort between 8–80 hours (8/80 Rule).
>
> ✅ **100% Rule** — WBS must represent 100% of project scope; sum of children = parent; project management included.
>
> ✅ **Three Types** — Deliverable-oriented, Phase-oriented, Responsibility-oriented; hybrids are common.
>
> ✅ **Code of Accounts** — Unique alphanumeric identifier for each WBS element (e.g., 1.3.2).
>
> ✅ **WBS Dictionary** — Document detailing scope, owner, schedule, cost, and quality for every WBS element.
>
> ✅ **Control Account** — Management control point grouping work packages for cost/schedule performance tracking.
>
> ✅ **Decomposition Principles** — Stop at work package level; each child = 100% of parent; mutually exclusive deliverables.
>
> ✅ **Downstream Linkages** — WBS feeds into Schedule, Cost Estimate, RAM, RBS, EVM, and Procurement Plan.
>
> ✅ **Exam Favourite Topics** — 100% Rule, 8/80 Rule, WBS Dictionary, types of WBS, drawing a WBS, comparison with project schedule.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Budgeting Process - Planning - Analysis - Selection - Financing - Implementation - Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capital Budgeting Process - Planning - Analysis - Selection - Financing - Implementation - Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Capital Budgeting

> [!NOTE]
> **Capital Budgeting** is the systematic process by which an organization evaluates, plans, and controls long-term investment projects (capital expenditures) that involve significant outflows of funds today in anticipation of cash inflows spread over multiple future periods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, capital budgeting is treated as the **financial backbone of project management** — the bridge between *strategic intent* (a project idea) and *operational reality* (an asset producing revenue). It is the process of allocating a firm's scarce long-term capital among competing investment proposals so that shareholder / stakeholder wealth is maximised.

### Key Vocabulary the Examiner Loves

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** | Funds deployed to acquire, upgrade, or replace long-term physical assets (land, plant, machinery, R&D). |
| **Working Capital** | Short-term assets minus short-term liabilities — also required to operationalise a capital project. |
| **Incremental Cash Flow** | The *difference* in cash flow between taking the project and not taking it. |
| **Sunk Cost** | An expenditure already incurred and unrecoverable — **must be ignored** in capital budgeting decisions. |
| **Opportunity Cost** | The benefit foregone by choosing one project over the next-best alternative. |
| **Cost of Capital (r)** | The minimum required rate of return demanded by investors / lenders; serves as the discount rate. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Dream-House Budget

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box: Buying a Home vs. Capital Budgeting**

Imagine a family with **₹50 Lakhs** in savings debating whether to build a duplex house (Project A) or a commercial shop (Project B). They wouldn't simply hand over the cheque the same evening. They would:

1. **Plan** — list every dream feature (rooms, parking, location).
2. **Analyse** — compare rents saved vs. shop income, EMI capacity, taxes.
3. **Select** — choose the option giving maximum *future benefit* per rupee spent.
4. **Finance** — arrange home loan, gold loan, or partner funds.
5. **Implement** — break ground, monitor construction, track milestones.
6. **Review** — after possession, ask "Was the actual rental yield what we projected?"

A firm does exactly the same thing — only the stakes are in **crores** and the language is **NPV, IRR, PI**. Capital budgeting is, in plain English, *corporate family budgeting for long-life assets*.

---

## 1.3 The Six Phases — Macro Map

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the capital budgeting process into **six sequential phases**:
>
> **Planning → Analysis → Selection → Financing → Implementation → Review**
>
> These phases are cyclic, not linear — the *Review* phase feeds back into the next round of *Planning*.

---

## 1.4 Why Capital Budgeting Matters in Engineering Projects

* **Irreversibility** — Once a ₹200 Cr cement plant is built, you cannot "undo" the decision easily.
* **Long gestation** — Cash outflows occur *now*; inflows may arrive 7–15 years later.
* **Strategic alignment** — A green-field EV battery plant ties the firm to a technological path for decades.
* **Risk & uncertainty** — Future cash flows are *estimates*, not certainties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** A capital budgeting decision is a **commitment of today's resources for tomorrow's returns** — and therefore the quality of the decision is judged by *ex-ante* discipline, not *ex-post* regret.

---

## 1.5 Types of Capital Budgeting Projects

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with these project classes:

1. **Replacement Projects** — Replacing worn-out equipment with newer, efficient technology.
2. **Expansion Projects** — Increasing output of an existing product line.
3. **New Product / New Market Projects** — Diversification into untested territory.
4. **Mandatory / Regulatory Projects** — Pollution-control equipment mandated by law; not evaluated on profit but on *least-cost compliance*.
5. **R&D / Strategic Projects** — Innovation spends whose benefits are difficult to quantify.
6. **Social / Welfare Projects** — Often undertaken by public-sector units on socio-economic criteria.

---

## 1.6 Snapshot of the Six Phases

| # | Phase | One-line Essence | Primary Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Planning** | Generate & screen investment ideas | Strategic alignment matrix |
| 2 | **Analysis** | Estimate cash flows & risk | DCF techniques (NPV, IRR) |
| 3 | **Selection** | Rank projects; accept / reject | NPV / PI / Payback criteria |
| 4 | **Financing** | Arrange funds optimally | WACC, Debt–Equity mix |
| 5 | **Implementation** | Execute with cost & time control | S-curve, Earned Value |
| 6 | **Review** | Post-completion audit | Variance analysis |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Phase 1 — PLANNING (Identification & Screening)

The planning phase is where *ideas become proposals*. Strategic objectives are translated into concrete investment opportunities.

### 2.1.1 Steps in Planning
1. **Identify investment opportunities** — linked to corporate strategy (Vision 2030, ESG goals, capacity augmentation).
2. **Classify** into Replacement / Expansion / New / Mandatory.
3. **Preliminary screening** using *qualitative* filters: strategic fit, technical feasibility, regulatory clearance.
4. **Prepare a Project Proposal** containing — purpose, scope, estimated cost, expected benefits, risk profile, proposed timeline.
5. **Budget envelope allocation** — total CapEx ceiling is fixed by the Board.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — "Strategic Alignment Score":** Many KTU-grade firms use a 1–5 scoring rubric across dimensions like *Market Growth, Competitive Position, Resource Fit, Risk*. Only proposals clearing a threshold (say ≥15/25) advance to Phase 2.

---

## 2.2 Phase 2 — ANALYSIS (Estimation & Evaluation)

This is the **mathematical heart** of capital budgeting. Three sub-tasks:

### Sub-task A — Estimate Cash Flows

The only cash flow that matters is the **Incremental After-Tax Cash Flow**:

$$
\text{Operating Cash Flow} = (\text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses} - \text{Depreciation}) \times (1 - t) + \text{Depreciation}
$$

where $t$ = corporate tax rate.

### Sub-task B — Determine the Discount Rate

The **Cost of Capital** $r$ is computed as the weighted average cost of debt, preference, and equity (WACC):

$$
r_{WACC} = \frac{E}{V} \, r_e + \frac{D}{V} \, r_d (1 - t) + \frac{P}{V} \, r_p
$$

where $V = E + D + P$ is the total market value of financing.

### Sub-task C — Apply Evaluation Techniques

Five techniques appear repeatedly in KTU question papers — see the formula sheet below.

---

## 2.3 Phase 3 — SELECTION (Ranking & Approval)

After NPV / IRR / PI are computed, the firm applies **decision rules**:

* **Independent projects** — Accept if NPV ≥ 0 (or PI ≥ 1, or IRR ≥ r).
* **Mutually exclusive projects** — Rank by **NPV first**, then by **PI** when capital is rationed.
* **Capital Rationing** — When the firm has a budget ceiling, use **Profitability Index** to pick the combination that maximises total NPV.
* **Qualitative override** — Strategic or regulatory reasons may override pure NPV.

---

## 2.4 Phase 4 — FINANCING (Capital Structure Decision)

Sources of long-term finance:

| Source | Cost | Risk to Firm | Dilution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equity (Shares)** | Highest ($r_e$) | Lowest (no obligation) | Yes |
| **Preference Shares** | Moderate ($r_p$) | Low (fixed dividend) | Partial |
| **Term Loans / Debentures** | Lowest pre-tax ($r_d$); moderate post-tax | High (interest obligation) | No |
| **Retained Earnings** | Opportunity cost = $r_e$ | None | No |
| **Leasing** | Implicit rate | Low | No |
| **Venture Capital / Mezzanine** | Very high | Moderate | Yes |

### Key Concept — *EBIT–EPS Indifference Analysis*

To choose between debt and equity, finance managers compute the **EBIT level** at which Earnings Per Share are equal under two financing plans:

$$
\frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1 - t) - PD_1}{N_1} = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1 - t) - PD_2}{N_2}
$$

where $I$ = interest, $PD$ = preference dividend, $N$ = number of equity shares.

---

## 2.5 Phase 5 — IMPLEMENTATION (Execution & Control)

Once funds are arranged, the project enters execution. Two control tools are central:

1. **S-Curve Analysis** — Plots planned vs. actual cumulative cost over time; a tool for earned-value tracking.
2. **Earned Value Management (EVM)**:
   * PV (Planned Value) — budgeted cost of work scheduled.
   * EV (Earned Value) — budgeted cost of work performed.
   * AC (Actual Cost) — actual cost of work performed.

$$
\text{Cost Variance (CV)} = EV - AC
$$

$$
\text{Schedule Variance (SV)} = EV - PV
$$

> **Note:** CV < 0 ⇒ cost overrun. SV < 0 ⇒ schedule slippage.

---

## 2.6 Phase 6 — REVIEW (Post-Completion Audit)

Also called **Post-Completion Audit (PCA)** or **Post-Implementation Review (PIR)**.

### Purpose
1. Compare **actual** cash flows with **projected** cash flows.
2. Identify causes of variance — scope creep, optimism bias, market shifts.
3. Build institutional learning for future projects.
4. Hold project sponsors accountable.

### Tools
* Variance analysis — Revenue, cost, NPV variance.
* Sensitivity analysis (post-hoc).
* Lessons-learned documentation.

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet** for capital budgeting problems. Memorise every row.

| # | Technique | Formula | Decision Rule (Accept if) | Strength / Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Payback Period (PP)** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | $\text{PP} \le \text{Target PP}$ | Simple, ignores TVM & cash flow beyond cutoff. |
| 2 | **Discounted Payback** | Sum discounted CFs until cumulative = 0 | $\le$ Target | Considers TVM, still ignores post-cutoff CF. |
| 3 | **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** | $\text{ARR} = \dfrac{\text{Avg Annual Profit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$ | $\text{ARR} \ge r$ | Uses accounting profit, not cash. |
| 4 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | $\text{NPV} \ge 0$ | Best technique; uses all CFs and TVM. |
| 5 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | NPV $= 0$, solve for $r$ | $r \ge \text{Cost of Capital}$ | May give multiple roots for non-conventional CFs. |
| 6 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{I_0}$ | $\text{PI} \ge 1$ | Best under capital rationing. |
| 7 | **WACC** | $r_{WACC} = \dfrac{E}{V} r_e + \dfrac{D}{V} r_d(1-t) + \dfrac{P}{V} r_p$ | Used as discount rate | Captures blended financing cost. |
| 8 | **Operating Cash Flow** | $\text{OCF} = (\text{Revenue} - \text{Exp} - \text{Dep})(1-t) + \text{Dep}$ | Project's annual CF | Adds back non-cash depreciation. |
| 9 | **Cost Variance** | $CV = EV - AC$ | $CV \ge 0$ favourable | EVM control tool. |
| 10 | **Schedule Variance** | $SV = EV - PV$ | $SV \ge 0$ favourable | EVM control tool. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Rule-of-Thumb Constants** used in KTU numericals (assume if not given):
> * Corporate tax rate $t =$ **30 %** (or **25 %** for new manufacturing companies).
> * Straight-line depreciation unless specified otherwise.
> * Project life typically **5 / 7 / 10 years**.
> * Salvage value at end of life — include in terminal cash flow.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering Projects

* **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP):** Government uses NPV to decide between Build-Operate-Transfer proposals.
* **Pharma R&D:** Firms use **Expected NPV** (incorporating probability of success) before committing to clinical trials.
* **Power Sector:** 25-year wind farm economics — NPV over the full asset life decides viability.
* **Software / IT:** Real-options valuation extends NPV by valuing the *option to abandon* or *expand*.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Present Value (NPV) Formula

### 3.1.1 Concept Setup
Consider a project requiring an initial investment $I_0$ at time $t = 0$ and generating cash flows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ at the end of years 1 through $n$. Let $r$ be the cost of capital.

**Step 1:** Discount each future cash flow back to present using the time-value-of-money principle:

$$
PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum all discounted cash flows to obtain the *present value of inflows*:

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the initial outflow at $t = 0$:

$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0
$$

This is the *expected addition to shareholder wealth* from the project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NPV is theoretically superior:** It assumes reinvestment of intermediate cash flows at the cost of capital $r$ (realistic), unlike IRR which implicitly assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Step 1:** Set NPV = 0:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} - I_0 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$
I_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for IRR (the discount rate that makes PV of inflows = Initial investment).

For an annuity (constant CF for $n$ years):

$$
I_0 = CF \times \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

Solving:

$$
r = \text{IRR} \quad \text{is the value of } r \text{ satisfying the above equality.}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** IRR is solved numerically (trial-and-error or interpolation). Direct algebraic solution is *only* possible for constant cash flows using the annuity formula. Show interpolation steps whenever you write IRR in an exam.

**Interpolation Formula:**

$$
\text{IRR} = r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)
$$

where $r_L$ = lower rate giving positive NPV, $r_H$ = higher rate giving negative NPV.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

Suppose the firm uses three sources — Equity (E), Debt (D), Preference (P).

**Step 1:** Identify cost of each source:
* $r_e$ = cost of equity
* $r_d$ = pre-tax cost of debt
* $r_p$ = cost of preference capital

**Step 2:** Compute weights by *market value* (book values only if market is unavailable):

$$
w_e = \frac{E}{E + D + P}, \quad w_d = \frac{D}{E + D + P}, \quad w_p = \frac{P}{E + D + P}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply after-tax adjustment to debt (interest is tax-deductible):

$$
\text{WACC} = w_e r_e + w_d r_d (1 - t) + w_p r_p
$$

**Step 4:** This WACC becomes the discount rate $r$ for NPV / PI computations.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full Capital Budgeting Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **14-mark KTU-style question** (you'll see it again in Section 5). Solve step-by-step here for clarity.

### Problem
*Project ABS* requires an initial outlay of **₹10,00,000**. Expected net after-tax cash inflows: **₹3,00,000 per year for 5 years**. Salvage value at end of year 5: **₹50,000**. Cost of capital: **10 %**. Tax rate already netted.

Compute **Payback Period, NPV, IRR, PI** and advise whether to accept.

---

### Solution Step-by-Step

#### (i) Payback Period

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}} = \frac{10,00,000}{3,00,000} = 3.33 \text{ years}
$$

#### (ii) Net Present Value

PV of annuity for 5 years at 10 %:

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 3,00,000 \times \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^5$:

$$
(1.10)^5 = 1.61051
$$

So $(1.10)^{-5} = 0.62092$.

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = 3,00,000 \times \frac{1 - 0.62092}{0.10} = 3,00,000 \times 3.7908 = \text{₹}11,37,240
$$

PV of salvage value:

$$
PV_{\text{salvage}} = \frac{50,000}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{50,000}{1.61051} = \text{₹}31,046
$$

Total PV of inflows:

$$
PV_{\text{total}} = 11,37,240 + 31,046 = \text{₹}11,68,286
$$

NPV:

$$
\text{NPV} = 11,68,286 - 10,00,000 = \text{₹}1,68,286
$$

#### (iii) Internal Rate of Return — by interpolation

Try $r = 15\,\%$:

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}}(15\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.15)^{-5}}{0.15} = \frac{1 - 0.4972}{0.15} = 3.3522
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}}(15\%) = 3,00,000 \times 3.3522 + \frac{50,000}{(1.15)^5}
$$

$$
= 10,05,660 + \frac{50,000}{2.0114} = 10,05,660 + 24,858 = 10,30,518
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{15\%} = 10,30,518 - 10,00,000 = +\text{₹}30,518
$$

Try $r = 16\,\%$:

$$
(1.16)^5 = 2.1003, \quad (1.16)^{-5} = 0.4761
$$

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}}(16\%, 5) = \frac{1 - 0.4761}{0.16} = 3.2743
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}}(16\%) = 3,00,000 \times 3.2743 + \frac{50,000}{2.1003}
$$

$$
= 9,82,290 + 23,806 = 10,06,096
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{16\%} = 10,06,096 - 10,00,000 = +\text{₹}6,096
$$

Try $r = 17\,\%$:

$$
(1.17)^5 \approx 2.1924
$$

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} = \frac{1 - (1.17)^{-5}}{0.17} = \frac{1 - 0.4561}{0.17} \approx 3.1992
$$

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} \approx 3,00,000 \times 3.1992 + \frac{50,000}{2.1924} = 9,59,760 + 22,807 = 9,82,567
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{17\%} = 9,82,567 - 10,00,000 = -\text{₹}17,433
$$

Since NPV changes sign between 16 % and 17 %, interpolate:

$$
\text{IRR} \approx 16\% + \frac{6,096}{6,096 - (-17,433)} \times (17\% - 16\%)
$$

$$
\text{IRR} \approx 16\% + \frac{6,096}{23,529} \times 1\% = 16\% + 0.259\% \approx 16.26\%
$$

#### (iv) Profitability Index

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{I_0} = \frac{11,68,286}{10,00,000} = 1.168
$$

### Decision
| Criterion | Value | Rule | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Payback | 3.33 yrs | ≤ 5 yrs | **Accept** |
| NPV | ₹1,68,286 | ≥ 0 | **Accept** |
| IRR | 16.26 % | ≥ 10 % | **Accept** |
| PI | 1.168 | ≥ 1 | **Accept** |

> **Conclusion:** *All four criteria suggest acceptance.* In KTU valuation, you must show **all** four computations for full marks — partial marks are awarded for each completed row of the decision matrix.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — NPV vs IRR Conflict

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: "Two projects have IRR > r but one has negative NPV." Resolution: **NPV dominates.** Why?

**Step 1:** Recall IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR; NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital $r$.

**Step 2:** In real life, intermediate cash flows can rarely be re-invested at the very high IRR — they are usually re-invested near $r$.

**Step 3:** Hence NPV's assumption is more realistic; **NPV is the primary decision metric** for mutually exclusive projects.

**Step 4:** Use IRR only as a *supplementary* communication tool (managers intuitively understand "16 % return").

---

## 3.6 Summary Equation Sheet (For Last-Minute Revision)

$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0
$$

$$
\text{IRR: NPV} = 0
$$

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{I_0}
$$

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}}
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = \frac{\text{Avg Annual Accounting Profit}}{I_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{WACC} = w_e r_e + w_d r_d(1-t) + w_p r_p
$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Complete Capital Budgeting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1 PLANNING] --> B[Phase 2 ANALYSIS]
    B --> C[Phase 3 SELECTION]
    C --> D[Phase 4 FINANCING]
    D --> E[Phase 5 IMPLEMENTATION]
    E --> F[Phase 6 REVIEW]
    F --> G{Lessons Learned}
    G --> A

    A1[Identify Opportunities] --> A
    A2[Classify Project Type] --> A
    A3[Strategic Screening] --> A
    A4[Prepare Proposal] --> A

    B1[Estimate Cash Flows] --> B
    B2[Compute Cost of Capital] --> B
    B3[Apply NPV IRR PI] --> B

    C1[Independent Project Decision] --> C
    C2[Mutually Exclusive Ranking] --> C
    C3[Capital Rationing Selection] --> C

    D1[Equity Issuance] --> D
    D2[Debt Arrangement] --> D
    D3[Internal Accruals] --> D

    E1[Execution Monitoring] --> E
    E2[Earned Value Tracking] --> E
    E3[Risk Response] --> E

    F1[Variance Analysis] --> F
    F2[Actual vs Projected CF] --> F
    F3[Audit and Accountability] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** The process is *cyclic* — Phase 6 (Review) loops back into Phase 1 (Planning) by feeding lessons learned into the next round of investment ideas. This feedback loop is what makes capital budgeting a *learning* system, not a one-time event.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of the Analysis Phase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inputs[Inputs Strategic Goals Market Data Technical Specs] --> Classifier[Classifier Project Type Replacement Expansion New Mandatory]
    Classifier --> CFEstimator[Cash Flow Estimator]
    CFEstimator --> Depreciation[Depreciation Module]
    CFEstimator --> TaxModule[Tax Adjustment]
    CFEstimator --> InflationMod[Inflation Module]

    CFEstimator --> OCF[Operating Cash Flow Series]

    OCF --> DiscountEngine[Discount Engine]
    WACCModule[Cost of Capital Module WACC] --> DiscountEngine

    DiscountEngine --> NPVCalc[NPV Calculator]
    DiscountEngine --> PICalc[PI Calculator]
    DiscountEngine --> IRRSolver[IRR Solver Newton Interpolation]

    NPVCalc --> DecisionMatrix[Decision Matrix]
    PICalc --> DecisionMatrix
    IRRSolver --> DecisionMatrix

    DecisionMatrix --> Output1[Accept]
    DecisionMatrix --> Output2[Reject]
    DecisionMatrix --> Output3[Defer]
    DecisionMatrix --> Output4[Modify Scope]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this:** This is a *block-level functional architecture* mapping how the Analysis phase is decomposed into four cooperating subsystems: (i) Cash-Flow Estimation, (ii) Cost-of-Capital Module, (iii) Discount Engine, and (iv) Decision Matrix. In real ERP systems (SAP, Oracle), this maps directly to capital-project management modules.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Project Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Evaluate Project] --> Q1{Are Projects Independent or Mutually Exclusive?}
    Q1 -->|Independent| Q2{NPV at least zero}
    Q1 -->|Mutually Exclusive| Q3{Rank by NPV}

    Q2 -->|Yes| Accept1[ACCEPT]
    Q2 -->|No| Reject1[REJECT]

    Q3 -->|Project A Highest NPV| SelectA[Select A]
    Q3 -->|Project B Highest NPV| SelectB[Select B]

    Start --> CapRation{Capital Rationed?}
    CapRation -->|Yes| UsePI[Rank by Profitability Index]
    CapRation -->|No| UseNPV[Rank by NPV]

    UsePI --> Budget[Stay within budget ceiling]
    UseNPV --> Budget
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the tree:**
> * **Independent projects** → Accept if NPV ≥ 0 (each evaluated alone).
> * **Mutually exclusive projects** → Pick the one with highest NPV.
> * **Capital rationing** → Use Profitability Index to maximise NPV per rupee of budget.
