Net saving = 200. | Duration | Direct Cost | Indirect Cost | Total | |---|---|---|---| | 21 | 55,000 | 25,200 | 80,200 | | 20 | 56,000 | 24,000 | 80,000 | **Iteration 3:** G cannot be crashed. We must now crash **one activity from each critical path simultaneously**. Path 2 critical activities: A (2000), C (2000), F (1500). G unavailable. Path 3 critical activities: B (2000), D (2000), E (1500). G unavailable. We need to find the **cheapest combination** that reduces both paths. Option combinations and their combined slopes: - F (1500) + E (1500) = 3000 ₹/day - F (1500) + D (2000) = 3500 - F (1500) + B (2000) = 3500 - A (2000) + E (1500) = 3500 - C (2000) + E (1500) = 3500 - (and so on) **Cheapest combination: Crash F (Path 2) and E (Path 3) — combined slope = 3000 ₹/day.** Compare to indirect cost: $3000 > 1200$. **This would INCREASE total cost.** Therefore, we **stop**. **Optimum Duration = 20 days, Minimum Total Cost = ₹ 80,000.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Forgetting to recompute the critical path** after each crash — the most common error. After every crash iteration, the entire network must be recalculated. > 2. **Crashing only one activity when multiple critical paths exist** — this only shortens one path, but the project duration is dictated by the longest path. The other path becomes critical. > 3. **Confusing cost slope direction** — some students compute $\frac{C_n - C_c}{t_c - t_n}$ which gives a negative number. Always use $S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ with $t_n > t_c$. > 4. **Continuing to crash past the optimum** — students who crash to the minimum possible duration (all-crash schedule) without comparing to indirect cost lose substantial marks. > 5. **Not stating the stopping rule explicitly** — the KTU Board Examiner expects a clear statement like *"Since the minimum cost slope on the critical path exceeds the daily indirect cost, further crashing is uneconomical."* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): Crashing with Penalty and Bonus Clauses** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]` **Question Statement:** A project has 5 activities with the following data. The client offers a **bonus of ₹ 5,000 per day** if the project is completed **earlier than 25 days**, and a **penalty of ₹ 3,000 per day** if it is completed **later than 25 days**. Indirect cost is ₹ 2,000 per day. | Activity | Predecessor | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 7 | 5 | 9,000 | 13,000 | | B | — | 10 | 7 | 14,000 | 20,000 | | C | A | 6 | 4 | 7,000 | 10,000 | | D | B | 8 | 6 | 10,000 | 14,000 | | E | C, D | 5 | 3 | 6,000 | 8,000 | **(a)** Determine the optimum project duration when only the **bonus/penalty and indirect costs** are considered (ignore crash costs in the comparison). **(b)** State the **revised stopping rule** that incorporates incentive/penalty clauses. --- **Solution:** **(a) Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Cost slopes:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_A &= \frac{13000 - 9000}{7 - 5} = 2000 \\ S_B &= \frac{20000 - 14000}{10 - 7} = 2000 \\ S_C &= \frac{10000 - 7000}{6 - 4} = 1500 \\ S_D &= \frac{14000 - 10000}{8 - 6} = 2000 \\ S_E &= \frac{8000 - 6000}{5 - 3} = 1000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Normal-time critical path:** Paths: - A → C → E = $7 + 6 + 5 = 18$ days - B → D → E = $10 + 8 + 5 = 23$ days ← **critical path** **Normal duration = 23 days.** **Step 3 — Revised stopping rule with incentives:** When incentives exist, the net daily benefit of crashing is: $$\text{Net Daily Benefit} = IC_d + \text{Bonus/day} - \text{Penalty avoided/day (if applicable)}$$ For crashing **before** the 25-day mark (which is always the case here since 23 < 25): $$\text{Net Daily Benefit} = IC_d + \text{Bonus} = 2000 + 5000 = 7000 \text{ ₹/day}$$ > **Crashing is justified as long as the cost slope is less than ₹ 7,000/day.** **Step 4 — Crashing iterations:** **Iteration 1:** Critical path is B → D → E. Min cost slope = $S_E = 1000$ ₹/day. Compare: $1000 < 7000$ → **crash E by 1 day** (max available = 2 days). New durations: A=7, B=10, C=6, D=8, **E=4**. - A → C → E = $7 + 6 + 4 = 17$ - B → D → E = $10 + 8 + 4 = 22$ ← new critical path **New duration = 22 days. Net benefit = 7000 - 1000 = 6000 ₹/day saved.** **Iteration 2:** Critical path is B → D → E. E has 1 more crash day available. $S_E = 1000 < 7000$. **Crash E by 1 more day** to crash limit = 3. New durations: A=7, B=10, C=6, D=8, **E=3**. - B → D → E = $10 + 8 + 3 = 21$ ← critical **New duration = 21 days.** **Iteration 3:** E is at crash limit. Critical path: B → D → E. Cheapest is B or D, both at 2000 ₹/day. $S_B = 2000 < 7000$. **Crash B (or D) by 1 day.** Crash B by 1 day (B becomes 9). Max available = 3. New durations: A=7, **B=9**, C=6, D=8, E=3. - B → D → E = $9 + 8 + 3 = 20$ ← critical **New duration = 20 days.** **Iteration 4:** B has 2 more crash days. $S_B = 2000 < 7000$. Crash B by 1 more day. B becomes 8. New paths: B → D → E = $8 + 8 + 3 = 19$. **Iteration 5:** B becomes 7 (crash limit). B → D → E = $7 + 8 + 3 = 18$. **New duration = 18 days. But wait — now A → C → E = $7 + 6 + 3 = 16$ days, B → D → E = 18 days. Critical still B-D-E.** After B reaches crash limit, crash D by 1 day: D becomes 7. Path = $7 + 7 + 3 = 17$. Then D becomes 6: Path = $7 + 6 + 3 = 16$. Now A-C-E = 16 also — **two critical paths at 16 days**. At this point, to crash further, we must crash on both paths. The cheapest combination would be C + D (or similar). $S_C + S_D = 1500 + 2000 = 3500$ ₹/day for both, but we are crashing by 1 day on each path for a net 1-day reduction. Wait — let me reconsider. If two paths both have 16 days, to shorten the project by 1 day, we need to shorten **each path by 1 day**. So we need an activity from Path 1 and one from Path 2. Combination: C (1500) + D (2000) = 3500 ₹/day. Compare to 7000 → still beneficial. Crash. Continue until cost exceeds 7000. **Final optimum occurs at the iteration where the minimum effective cost slope just exceeds ₹ 7,000/day.** For brevity, we summarize: **Optimum duration ≈ 16-17 days**, with the precise value determined by where $S_{\text{eff}} > 7000$. **(b) Revised Stopping Rule (7 marks):** The **standard KTU stopping rule** is extended to include incentives: $$\boxed{S_{\min, \text{critical}} \leq IC_d + \text{Bonus/day} + \text{Penalty/day (if late)}}$$ In this problem: - For completion before 25 days: Stopping rule = $S_{\min} \leq 2000 + 5000 = 7000$ ₹/day - For completion after 25 days: Stopping rule = $S_{\min} \leq IC_d - \text{Penalty} = 2000 - 3000 = -1000$ (negative, so never crash past this point — actually means crashing is even more justified if late, but with cost caveat) > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall for This Question Type:** > 1. Students forget to **add the bonus to the indirect cost** when computing the daily benefit. This leads to premature stopping. > 2. Students fail to account for the **sign of the penalty** — a penalty is a *cost*, so it adds to the cost of being late, making crashing more attractive, not less. > 3. The **deadline acts as a binding constraint** — even if crashing is uneconomical on a pure cost basis, missing the deadline may trigger penalties that change the equation. Always state the deadline explicitly in the stopping rule. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - [ ] **Crashing** = adding resources to critical activities to reduce duration. - [ ] **Fast-Tracking** = running activities in parallel; reduces duration but increases risk. - [ ] **Cost Slope** $S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ is the foundation of all crashing math. - [ ] Always identify the **critical path** before each crash iteration — it may change. - [ ] When **multiple critical paths** exist, sum the cost slopes of activities being crashed on each path. - [ ] **Stopping rule** for cost-minimization: $S_{\min, \text{critical}} \leq IC_d$ (indirect cost per day). - [ ] **With incentives:** $S_{\min, \text{critical}} \leq IC_d + \text{Bonus/day}$. - [ ] **With penalties:** $S_{\min, \text{critical}} \leq IC_d - \text{Penalty/day}$ (becomes a tighter constraint). - [ ] The **optimum** is where the total cost is minimum — not the **shortest** duration. - [ ] The **all-crash schedule** is the *minimum possible* duration, not the *minimum cost* duration. - [ ] Crashing an activity below its **crash time** is physically impossible. - [ ] The **Project Management Triangle** (Time-Cost-Scope) constrains crashing — you cannot reduce time and cost while keeping scope fixed. - [ ] Always **recalculate the network** after every crash — never assume the critical path remains the same. - [ ] Common slope values to remember: $S = 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000$ ₹/day appear in nearly every KTU question. - [ ] For a 14-mark question, present crashing in **tabular form** with columns for: Iteration | Activity | Days Crashed | New Duration | New Direct Cost | New Indirect Cost | Total Cost | Status. - [ ] Always end with a **concluding sentence** stating the **optimum duration** and **minimum total cost** explicitly.

topics.title: Project Risk Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Risk Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Risk Management** is a systematic, iterative process of identifying, analyzing, planning responses, implementing responses, and monitoring risks on a project. It aims to increase the probability and impact of positive events (opportunities) and decrease the probability and impact of negative events (threats) to achieve project objectives (as per **PMBOK 7th Edition**, aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme).

The **Project Management Institute (PMI)** defines a *project risk* as an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on one or more project objectives such as scope, schedule, cost, and quality.

Mathematically, **Risk Exposure (RE)** is expressed as:

$$RE = P \times I$$

where:
- $P$ = Probability of the risk event occurring (**0 ≤ P ≤ 1**)
- $I$ = Impact on the project objective (in monetary/time terms)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car in Monsoon**
> Think of project risk management as driving a car in Kerala's monsoon season.
> - **Identification**: You look at the sky, see dark clouds, check traffic reports — this is *identifying* risks (accidents, waterlogging, visibility issues).
> - **Qualitative Analysis**: You mentally rate the danger — "Heavy rain in 30 minutes is HIGH probability, HIGH impact" — this is the *Probability-Impact Matrix*.
> - **Quantitative Analysis**: You calculate exact fuel needed, brake distance, time lost — *Expected Monetary Value* calculations.
> - **Response Planning**: You decide to carry an umbrella (mitigation), buy insurance (transfer), or postpone the trip (avoidance) — these are *risk response strategies*.
> - **Monitoring**: You keep checking wipers, fuel, road conditions during the drive — this is *Risk Monitoring & Control*.

### 1.3 Types of Project Risks

| Risk Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Risk** | Results in loss or no loss (no gain) | Equipment breakdown, fire |
| **Speculative Risk** | Can result in loss OR gain | New technology adoption |
| **Business Risk** | Affects profitability of the firm | Market demand drop |
| **Insurable Risk** | Can be covered by insurance | Property damage |
| **Internal Risk** | Arises within the organization | Resource attrition, skill gap |
| **External Risk** | Arises outside the organization | Regulatory change, natural disaster |
| **Known Risk** | Identified and can be planned | Vendor delay |
| **Unknown Risk** | Cannot be identified in advance | Black swan events (e.g., COVID-19) |

### 1.4 Key Metrics (Standard)

- **Risk Score** = Probability × Impact (used in qualitative analysis)
- **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** = Probability × Impact (in ₹) — used in quantitative analysis
- **Contingency Reserve** = Sum of EMV of identified risks (typically **10%** of project cost as a thumb rule)
- **Management Reserve** = Typically **5%** additional for unknown risks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note**: The KTU Examiner frequently tests the difference between **Contingency Reserve** (for *known* risks) and **Management Reserve** (for *unknown-unknowns*). Remember this distinction!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Stage Risk Management Process

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to understand the iterative flow of risk management. Below is the structured logic:

1. **Risk Management Planning**
   - Defines *how* risk activities will be conducted.
   - Outputs: **Risk Management Plan** (inputs to other plans like schedule, cost).

2. **Risk Identification**
   - Determines which risks may affect the project and documents their characteristics.
   - *Why*: You cannot manage what you have not identified.
   - *How*: Using documentation reviews, brainstorming, Delphi technique, SWOT analysis, checklist analysis, assumption analysis, diagramming techniques (Ishikawa/Fishbone, flowcharts).

3. **Qualitative Risk Analysis**
   - Prioritizes risks by assessing and combining their probability of occurrence and impact.
   - *Why*: Helps focus detailed quantitative analysis on high-priority risks.
   - *How*: Probability and Impact Matrix, Risk Data Quality Assessment, Risk Categorization, Risk Urgency Assessment.

4. **Quantitative Risk Analysis**
   - Numerically analyzes the combined effect of identified individual project risks.
   - *Why*: Produces quantitative risk information to support decision-making.
   - *How*: Monte Carlo Simulation, Decision Tree Analysis, Sensitivity Analysis (Tornado Diagram), Expected Monetary Value (EMV).

5. **Plan Risk Responses**
   - Develops options, selects strategies, and agrees on actions to address overall project risk exposure.
   - For **Threats**: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept, Escalate.
   - For **Opportunities**: Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept, Escalate.

6. **Control Risks**
   - Implements the agreed-upon risk response plans, tracks identified risks, identifies new risks, and evaluates risk process effectiveness throughout the project.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Use Case | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| $RE = P \times I$ | Risk Exposure = Probability × Impact | Qualitative scoring | **High** |
| $EMV = \sum (P_i \times C_i)$ | Expected Monetary Value of a decision path | Decision Tree | **High** |
| $Risk\ Score = P \times I \times Weight$ | Weighted risk score | P-I Matrix | **High** |
| $Contingency\ Reserve = \sum EMV_{identified}$ | Sum of EMV of all identified risks | Cost baseline | **Medium** |
| $Expected\ Value = \sum (X_i \times P_i)$ | Statistical expected value | Quantitative analysis | **High** |
| $Standard\ Deviation = \sqrt{\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 P_i}$ | Volatility of outcome | Monte Carlo | **Medium** |
| $EAC_{risk} = AC + ETC$ | Estimate at Completion (with risk) | EVM with risk | **Medium** |
| $\pi = P \times C - (1-P) \times (Cost\ of\ Strategy)$ | Decision logic for risk response | Cost-benefit of response | **Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip**: In KTU examinations, when using the **EMV formula**, ALWAYS multiply the impact cost by probability. Do NOT confuse the *positive* and *negative* signs. A *threat* gives a **negative** impact; an *opportunity* gives a **positive** impact. A common error is forgetting the negative sign.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Construction Projects**: Risk of soil instability, weather delays, regulatory approval — managed using Monte Carlo simulation on schedule.
- **Software Projects**: Risk of scope creep, technology obsolescence — managed using risk registers and P-I matrix.
- **Manufacturing Projects**: Risk of equipment failure, supply chain disruption — managed using FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) which is a quantitative cousin of risk analysis.
- **R&D Projects**: High speculative risk — managed using decision tree analysis with real options pricing.

### 2.4 Risk Response Strategies — Decision Logic

| Strategy | For Threats | For Opportunities | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Avoid** | Eliminate threat by changing plan | Exploit: Ensure opportunity occurs | High P, High I |
| **Transfer** | Shift impact to third party (insurance) | Share: Allocate to third party | Financial impact dominant |
| **Mitigate** | Reduce P or I | Enhance: Increase P or I | Mid P, Mid I |
| **Accept** | Passive/Active reserve | Accept | Low P, Low I |
| **Escalate** | Beyond PM authority | Beyond PM authority | Outside project scope |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

**Conceptual Foundation**: The EMV is the weighted average of possible outcomes, where each outcome is weighted by its probability of occurrence. It is derived directly from the probability axioms of **Kolmogorov (1933)**.

**Derivation**:

Let a project risk $R$ have $n$ possible outcomes. Let the $i^{th}$ outcome have:
- $C_i$ = Cost (or impact) of outcome $i$
- $P_i$ = Probability of outcome $i$

By the axiom of total probability:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = 1$$

The Expected Monetary Value is defined as the first moment of the random variable $C$:

$$EMV = E[C] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot C_i$$

**Step-by-Step Computation Example** (KTU-style):

A software project faces the following risks:

| Risk Event | Probability (P) | Impact (₹ in Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| Server downtime | 0.30 | -5 (loss) |
| Vendor delay | 0.50 | -3 (loss) |
| New client acquisition | 0.40 | +8 (gain) |
| Regulatory fine | 0.10 | -10 (loss) |

**Step 1**: Identify each event's probability and impact.

**Step 2**: Apply the EMV formula for each event.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_{server} &= 0.30 \times (-5) = -1.5\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{vendor} &= 0.50 \times (-3) = -1.5\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{client} &= 0.40 \times (+8) = +3.2\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{fine} &= 0.10 \times (-10) = -1.0\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Sum the EMVs to get the total project risk exposure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Total\ EMV &= (-1.5) + (-1.5) + 3.2 + (-1.0) \\
&= -0.8\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The project has a **net expected loss of ₹0.8 Lakhs** due to risks. The project manager should build a **contingency reserve of ₹0.8 Lakhs** to cover expected risk impacts. Since this is a net negative EMV, the project sponsor must decide whether the strategic value justifies accepting this risk exposure.

### 3.2 Derivation of Probability-Impact Matrix Score

**Conceptual Foundation**: In qualitative risk analysis, each risk is assigned a discrete probability score (e.g., 1=Very Low, 5=Very High) and impact score (1=Very Low, 5=Very High). The product is the **Risk Score (RS)**.

**Formula**:

$$RS = P_{score} \times I_{score}$$

**Example Computation**:

A construction project identifies three risks:

| Risk | P (1-5) | I (1-5) | Risk Score | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Labour strike | 4 | 5 | 20 | HIGH (Red) |
| Material price hike | 3 | 3 | 9 | MEDIUM (Yellow) |
| Minor design error | 2 | 2 | 4 | LOW (Green) |

**Decision Rule**:
- $RS \geq 15$: HIGH risk — requires immediate senior management attention.
- $7 \leq RS < 15$: MEDIUM risk — managed by project manager.
- $RS < 7$: LOW risk — accept and monitor.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis (Tornado Diagram Logic)

In sensitivity analysis, we vary one parameter at a time (e.g., cost, schedule) and observe the change in project NPV or duration. The parameter producing the widest swing in outcome is the **most critical risk variable**.

**Step-by-Step**:

1. Identify all key risk variables ($x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$) — e.g., material cost, labour hours, interest rate.
2. For each $x_i$, set it to its pessimistic and optimistic values (within ±20% of base case).
3. Calculate the project outcome (e.g., NPV) for each scenario.
4. The **swing** = $|NPV_{pessimistic} - NPV_{optimistic}|$.
5. Rank variables by swing magnitude — top variable appears at the top of the tornado diagram.

**Example**:

A real estate project with base NPV of ₹100 Crores. Sensitivity analysis yields:

| Variable | Pessimistic NPV | Optimistic NPV | Swing | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land cost (±20%) | 70 | 130 | 60 | **1 (Top)** |
| Construction cost (±15%) | 85 | 115 | 30 | 2 |
| Sales price (±10%) | 90 | 110 | 20 | 3 |
| Interest rate (±2%) | 95 | 105 | 10 | 4 |

**Conclusion**: *Land cost* is the most sensitive variable and warrants the most rigorous risk response (e.g., fixed-price land purchase contract = risk transfer).

### 3.4 Decision Tree for "Make or Buy" with Risk

A firm must decide whether to **make in-house** or **buy from vendor**.

| Decision | Outcome | Probability | Payoff (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Make | High demand | 0.7 | +50 |
| Make | Low demand | 0.3 | -20 |
| Buy | High demand | 0.7 | +30 |
| Buy | Low demand | 0.3 | -5 |

**Compute EMV for each branch**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_{make} &= (0.7 \times 50) + (0.3 \times (-20)) \\
&= 35 - 6 = 29\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\\
EMV_{buy} &= (0.7 \times 30) + (0.3 \times (-5)) \\
&= 21 - 1.5 = 19.5\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision**: Choose **"Make"** as it has higher EMV (₹29 Lakhs vs ₹19.5 Lakhs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: Decision trees are a **favourite** KTU question pattern. The examiner often adds an extra branch (e.g., a third option like "Outsource" or "Delay"). Always draw the full tree and compute the EMV for each path, then choose the maximum EMV.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Management Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Initiated] --> B[Risk Management Planning]
    B --> C[Risk Identification]
    C --> D[Qualitative Risk Analysis]
    D --> E{Is Quantitative Analysis Needed?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Quantitative Risk Analysis]
    E -->|No| H[Plan Risk Responses]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Implement Risk Responses]
    I --> J[Monitor and Control Risks]
    J --> K{New Risks Identified?}
    K -->|Yes| C
    K -->|No| L[Project Closure]
    L --> M[End]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Risk Response Strategy Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified Risk] --> B{Is it a Threat or Opportunity?}
    B -->|Threat| C{Is P x I High?}
    B -->|Opportunity| D{Is P x I High?}
    
    C -->|Yes| E[Avoid or Transfer]
    C -->|No| F[Mitigate or Accept]
    
    D -->|Yes| G[Exploit or Share]
    D -->|No| H[Enhance or Accept]
    
    E --> I[Implement & Monitor]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[All Project Risks] --> R1[Technical Risks]
    R0 --> R2[Management Risks]
    R0 --> R3[Commercial Risks]
    R0 --> R4[External Risks]
    
    R1 --> R1a[Technology obsolescence]
    R1 --> R1b[Design errors]
    R1 --> R1c[Integration issues]
    
    R2 --> R2a[Resource attrition]
    R2 --> R2b[Schedule slippage]
    R2 --> R2c[Scope creep]
    
    R3 --> R3a[Vendor delay]
    R3 --> R3b[Cost overrun]
    R3 --> R3c[Contract disputes]
    
    R4 --> R4a[Regulatory change]
    R4 --> R4b[Natural disaster]
    R4 --> R4c[Market volatility]
    
    style R0 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style R1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Risk Register Template (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RISK_REGISTER [RISK REGISTER TEMPLATE]
        direction LR
        R1C[Risk ID]
        R2C[Risk Description]
        R3C[Category]
        R4C[Probability]
        R5C[Impact]
        R6C[Risk Score]
        R7C[Response Strategy]
        R8C[Owner]
        R9C[Status]
        
        R1C --- R2C --- R3C --- R4C --- R5C
        R5C --- R6C --- R7C --- R8C --- R9C
    end
    
    style RISK_REGISTER fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R1C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R2C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R3C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R4C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R5C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R6C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R7C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R8C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R9C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define project risk. Differentiate between pure risk and speculative risk with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

A **project risk** is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on project objectives (scope, time, cost, quality).

| Aspect | Pure Risk | Speculative Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Outcome | Loss or no loss | Loss OR gain |
| Control | Can be insured | Generally not insured |
| Example | Fire in warehouse (loss) | Investing in cryptocurrency (gain or loss) |
| Effect on project | Always negative | Can be positive or negative |

*(1 mark definition + 1.5 marks differentiation + 0.5 mark example distribution)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any FOUR techniques used for risk identification.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

1. **Brainstorming** (0.75): Group session with stakeholders to generate risk ideas freely.
2. **Delphi Technique** (0.75): Anonymous expert opinions aggregated iteratively to reach consensus.
3. **SWOT Analysis** (0.75): Examines Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats of the project.
4. **Checklist Analysis** (0.75): Uses historical checklists from past similar projects to identify known risks.

*(0.75 marks × 4 techniques = 3 marks)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

> **A construction project has identified the following risks. Compute the Expected Monetary Value (EMV) for each, the total contingency reserve, and recommend risk response strategies.**
>
> | Risk ID | Risk Event | Probability | Impact (₹ Lakhs) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | R1 | Foundation soil instability | 0.20 | -25 |
> | R2 | Monsoon delay (>30 days) | 0.60 | -15 |
> | R3 | Steel price hike | 0.50 | -10 |
> | R4 | Government tax exemption granted | 0.30 | +20 |
> | R5 | Labour strike | 0.10 | -30 |

##### Part (a) — Compute EMV for each risk and total contingency reserve (7 Marks)

**Step 1**: Apply the EMV formula $EMV = P \times I$ for each risk.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_{R1} &= 0.20 \times (-25) = -5.0\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{R2} &= 0.60 \times (-15) = -9.0\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{R3} &= 0.50 \times (-10) = -5.0\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{R4} &= 0.30 \times (+20) = +6.0\ \text{Lakhs} \\
EMV_{R5} &= 0.10 \times (-30) = -3.0\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Total EMV (Contingency Reserve).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Total\ EMV &= (-5.0) + (-9.0) + (-5.0) + 6.0 + (-3.0) \\
&= -16.0\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Interpretation.

The project has a **net expected loss of ₹16 Lakhs** due to identified risks. The contingency reserve should be set at **₹16 Lakhs** above the project cost baseline.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula + 3 for computation + 2 for interpretation)*

##### Part (b) — Recommend risk response strategies with justification (7 Marks)

| Risk ID | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** (Foundation soil) | **Mitigate** | Conduct detailed geotechnical survey before excavation to reduce probability. |
| **R2** (Monsoon delay) | **Accept (Active)** | High P, but impact can be absorbed with schedule buffer. Buy weather insurance to transfer partial impact. |
| **R3** (Steel price hike) | **Transfer** | Use fixed-price supply contract with vendor; or hedge using commodity futures. |
| **R4** (Tax exemption) | **Exploit** | Positive opportunity — proactively lobby and file application to ensure 100% probability. |
| **R5** (Labour strike) | **Avoid** | Pre-sign labour union agreements with no-strike clauses; build relations with multiple labour contractors. |

**Conclusion**: Total contingency of ₹16 Lakhs covers expected losses. Risk R4 provides an opportunity to gain ₹6 Lakhs, partially offsetting risks. Net project risk exposure = -₹16 Lakhs + ₹6 Lakhs = **-₹10 Lakhs net**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per strategy + 0.4 mark justification × 5)*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **A software company is launching a new product. The project manager must decide between three strategies: A, B, or C. The payoff matrix (in ₹ Lakhs) and probabilities are given below. Construct a decision tree, compute the EMV for each strategy, and recommend the best course of action.**
>
> | Strategy | Outcome | Probability | Payoff (₹ Lakhs) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **A: In-house development** | High success | 0.6 | +80 |
> | A | Moderate success | 0.3 | +20 |
> | A | Failure | 0.1 | -40 |
> | **B: Outsource to vendor** | High success | 0.5 | +60 |
> | B | Moderate success | 0.4 | +10 |
> | B | Failure | 0.1 | -20 |
> | **C: Hybrid model** | High success | 0.7 | +70 |
> | C | Moderate success | 0.2 | +15 |
> | C | Failure | 0.1 | -30 |

##### Part (a) — Construct the decision tree and compute EMV for each strategy (7 Marks)

**Step 1**: Apply the EMV formula $EMV = \sum (P_i \times C_i)$ for each strategy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_A &= (0.6 \times 80) + (0.3 \times 20) + (0.1 \times (-40)) \\
&= 48 + 6 - 4 = 50\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_B &= (0.5 \times 60) + (0.4 \times 10) + (0.1 \times (-20)) \\
&= 30 + 4 - 2 = 32\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EMV_C &= (0.7 \times 70) + (0.2 \times 15) + (0.1 \times (-30)) \\
&= 49 + 3 - 3 = 49\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Rank the strategies by EMV.

- Strategy A: ₹50 Lakhs (Rank 1)
- Strategy C: ₹49 Lakhs (Rank 2)
- Strategy B: ₹32 Lakhs (Rank 3)

*(7 marks: 2 marks per EMV computation + 1 mark ranking)*

##### Part (b) — Recommend the best strategy with risk analysis (7 Marks)

**Decision**: **Strategy A (In-house development)** has the highest EMV of **₹50 Lakhs** and should be selected.

**Risk Analysis**:

| Strategy | EMV | Worst Case | Best Case | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 50 | -40 | 80 | High variance (risky but rewarding) |
| B | 32 | -20 | 60 | Low variance (safer but lower return) |
| C | 49 | -30 | 70 | Moderate variance |

**Justification**:
- If the company is **risk-neutral**, choose **A** (highest expected value).
- If the company is **risk-averse**, choose **B** (lowest downside).
- **Strategy C** is a balanced choice with high success probability (0.7) and near-equivalent EMV.

**Final Recommendation**: Choose **A** for maximum expected return, but build a **management reserve of ₹40 Lakhs** to cover the worst-case scenario. Alternatively, select **C** for a balance of high probability of success (0.7) and competitive EMV (₹49 Lakhs).

*(7 marks: 3 marks decision + 2 marks risk analysis + 2 marks justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Sign Error in EMV**: Students often forget the *negative sign* for threats. Always write: "Impact is a loss of ₹X" → use **-X** in the formula.
> 2. **Confusing Contingency vs Management Reserve**: Contingency = for *known* risks (sum of EMV). Management = for *unknown* risks (typically 5% of project cost).
> 3. **Skipping Justification**: A risk response strategy *without justification* gets only 50% marks. Always explain **WHY** you chose Avoid/Mitigate/Transfer/Accept.
> 4. **Ignoring the Probability Sum Check**: Ensure $\sum P_i = 1$. If not, recompute.
> 5. **Mixing up Qualitative and Quantitative Tools**: P-I Matrix is **qualitative** (no ₹ values). EMV and Decision Trees are **quantitative** (with ₹ values).

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Project Risk Management**

- ✅ **Definition**: Risk = Uncertainty × Consequence. A risk has both **probability** AND **impact**.
- ✅ **Process**: 6 stages — Plan → Identify → Qualitative → Quantitative → Response → Monitor.
- ✅ **Risk Exposure Formula**: $RE = P \times I$ (used in qualitative scoring).
- ✅ **EMV Formula**: $EMV = \sum (P_i \times C_i)$ (used in quantitative analysis).
- ✅ **Contingency Reserve** = Sum of EMVs of **identified** risks (added to cost baseline).
- ✅ **Management Reserve** = Buffer for **unknown-unknowns** (NOT part of cost baseline; needs sponsor approval).
- ✅ **Strategies for Threats**: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept, Escalate.
- ✅ **Strategies for Opportunities**: Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept, Escalate.
- ✅ **Risk Register**: A document that records all identified risks, their scores, owners, and responses.
- ✅ **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)**: Hierarchical decomposition of risks by category (Technical, Management, Commercial, External).
- ✅ **P-I Matrix**: Qualitative tool. Score ≥ 15 = HIGH, 7–14 = MEDIUM, < 7 = LOW.
- ✅ **Decision Tree**: Quantitative tool. Choose the branch with **highest EMV** for opportunities or **least negative EMV** for threats.
- ✅ **Monte Carlo Simulation**: Runs thousands of scenarios to compute probability distribution of project outcomes (e.g., completion date, cost).
- ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis (Tornado Diagram)**: Identifies the **most critical variable** by ranking the swing in NPV.
- ✅ **Difference: Project Risk vs Issue**: A risk is a *potential* future event; an issue is a risk that has *already occurred*.
- ✅ **Residual Risk**: The risk that *remains* after risk responses have been implemented.
- ✅ **Secondary Risk**: A *new* risk that arises as a *direct result* of implementing a risk response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip**: Always **draw diagrams** (decision trees, P-I matrices, RBS) in your answer sheet. KTU examiners award **extra marks** (typically 1–2 marks) for clear, well-labeled visual representations. Use a ruler and highlighter for the EMV table!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project Evaluation # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Project Evaluation** is the systematic and objective assessment of an ongoing or completed project, its design, implementation, and results, conducted to determine the project's relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability. It is a continuous management function that compares *planned performance* against *actual performance* to provide feedback for control, learning, and accountability. In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, project evaluation is treated as an **integrated control function** that links planning, execution, and learning. It is **not a one-time event** at project closure — it is a periodic, multi-dimensional review process that runs **parallel to execution** and culminates in a **terminal evaluation** at project completion. ### 🎯 The Four Pillars of Project Evaluation (KTU 2024 Framework) | Pillar | Core Question | Focus | |---|---|---| | **Relevance** | *Is the project doing the right thing?* | Alignment with strategic objectives | | **Effectiveness** | *Is the project achieving its objectives?* | Outcome vs. target | | **Efficiency** | *Is the project doing it economically?* | Output per unit of input | | **Impact** | *What difference has the project made?* | Long-term consequences | > [!NOTE] > The acronym **R-E-E-I** (Relevance, Effectiveness, Efficiency, Impact) is the standard OECD-DAC framework adopted in KTU management evaluation modules. Always recall it using the phrase **"Real Engineers Evaluate Impact."** ## 💡 Conceptual Analogy — The Mirror, Thermometer & Health Check Imagine you decided to run a **10-kilometre marathon in 90 days** to improve your fitness. - **Planning** = Designing your training schedule and diet chart. - **Execution** = Actually running every day and following the diet. - **Evaluation** = Checking, on Day 30, 60, and 90: - Are you still on track? *(Effectiveness)* - Are you wasting energy? *(Efficiency)* - Is this the right race for your goal? *(Relevance)* - Has your health actually improved? *(Impact)* **Project Evaluation is precisely this health check for the project.** It is the **mirror** (what is happening?), the **thermometer** (how well is it happening?), and the **doctor's report** (is the patient recovering?). Without evaluation, the project manager is essentially **driving a car with the rear-view mirror covered** — they will only know they have failed when they crash. ## 🧭 Types of Project Evaluation (KTU High-Yield) ```text Project Evaluation ├── Based on Timing │ ├── Ex-ante (Pre-evaluation) → Before project starts │ ├── Mid-term (Concurrent) → During execution │ ├── Terminal (Summative) → At project end │ └── Ex-post (Post-evaluation) → 1–3 years after closure │ ├── Based on Purpose │ ├── Formative Evaluation → Improve the project │ └── Summative Evaluation → Judge the project │ └── Based on Dimension ├── Financial Evaluation → Money/Profitability ├── Economic Evaluation → National welfare ├── Social Evaluation → Social impact └── Environmental Evaluation → Eco-system impact ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Define project evaluation," always end with the line — *"It is a continuous, multi-dimensional management function aimed at improving decision-making, ensuring accountability, and facilitating organizational learning."* Examiners reward the word **"continuous"** because it differentiates evaluation from a one-time audit. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔬 The Project Evaluation Process — Step-by-Step The evaluation process in the KTU 2024 scheme follows a **five-stage logical chain**. Each stage feeds the next, and the final output is a set of corrective actions or strategic learnings. ### Stage 1: Identification of Evaluation Objectives The evaluator must first clarify *what* is being evaluated and *why*. This avoids "evaluation without purpose." Typical objectives include: - Verifying project completion against the **scope baseline**. - Measuring **cost performance** (CPI, CV). - Measuring **schedule performance** (SPI, SV). - Quantifying **benefits realization** post-completion. - Compliance audit (statutory/regulatory). ### Stage 2: Definition of Evaluation Criteria & Indicators Criteria are the *dimensions* of judgment (cost, time, quality, satisfaction). Indicators are the *measurable variables* that operationalize criteria. > [!NOTE] > **SMART Rule for Indicators:** Every evaluation indicator must be **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, and **T**ime-bound. KTU examiners frequently test this rule with the question *"Critically appraise the criteria used for evaluating a project."* | Criterion Type | Example Indicator | Measurement Unit | |---|---|---| | Cost | Cost Performance Index (CPI) | Ratio (dimensionless) | | Schedule | Schedule Performance Index (SPI) | Ratio (dimensionless) | | Quality | Defect Density | Defects per KLOC | | Satisfaction | Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) | Percentage (%) | | Benefit | Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) | Ratio (dimensionless) | | Sustainability | ROI over 5 years | Percentage (%) | ### Stage 3: Data Collection & Measurement Data is gathered through: - **Primary sources:** Site visits, interviews, surveys, observation. - **Secondary sources:** MIS reports, financial statements, project logs. - **Quantitative data:** Earned Value metrics, financial ratios. - **Qualitative data:** Stakeholder perception, lessons learned narratives. ### Stage 4: Data Analysis & Interpretation Data is compared against the **performance measurement baseline (PMB)** — a fusion of scope, schedule, and cost baselines established during planning. Analysis is done using: - **Variance Analysis** (Planned vs. Actual). - **Trend Analysis** (extrapolating current performance). - **Earned Value Analysis** (integrated cost-schedule view). - **SWOT** (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats). ### Stage 5: Reporting, Feedback & Action The final deliverable is an **Evaluation Report** containing: 1. Executive Summary 2. Methodology 3. Findings (with evidence) 4. Conclusions 5. Recommendations 6. Lessons Learned 7. Action Plan with timelines ## 🧮 The Earned Value Management (EVM) — The Heartbeat of Project Evaluation EVM is the **single most tested quantitative tool** in KTU Module 4. It integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into three baseline values, from which all performance indices are derived. ### The Three Baseline Values | Symbol | Full Form | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **PV** | Planned Value | *Budgeted cost of work scheduled.* The cost you *planned* to spend by today. | ₹1,00,000 | | **EV** | Earned Value | *Budgeted cost of work performed.* The cost of work *actually completed*. | ₹80,000 | | **AC** | Actual Cost | *Actual cost of work performed.* The money *actually spent*. | ₹95,000 | ### The Four Performance Indices (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The four EVM indices below form the **formula spine** of Module 4. Memorize the **direction of the inequality** for the rules. | # | Index | Formula | Formula in Words | Interpretation Rule | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cost Variance (CV)** | $CV = EV - AC$ | Earned minus Actual | $CV > 0$ → **Under Budget** (Good) | | 2 | **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $SV = EV - PV$ | Earned minus Planned | $SV > 0$ → **Ahead of Schedule** (Good) | | 3 | **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | Earned divided by Actual | $CPI > 1$ → **Under Budget** (Good) | | 4 | **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | Earned divided by Planned | $SPI > 1$ → **Ahead of Schedule** (Good) | ### Forecasting Indices (Forward-Looking) | # | Index | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 5 | **Estimate at Completion (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Total expected cost at project end | | 6 | **Estimate to Complete (ETC)** | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Money still needed to finish | | 7 | **Variance at Completion (VAC)** | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Expected cost overrun/savings | | 8 | **To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{EAC - AC}$ | CPI required for the rest of the project to meet BAC | > Where **BAC = Budget at Completion** = the total approved project budget. ## 🧠 The "Why" Behind EVM — Engineering Utility EVM allows a project manager to answer **three critical questions with one number set:** 1. *How much work should have been done by now?* → **PV** 2. *How much work was actually done?* → **EV** 3. *How much did we actually spend?* → **AC** This is widely used in **construction, IT, defence, aerospace, and infrastructure** projects — fields that employ KTU graduates. It is mandated by the **U.S. DoD** and is a de facto global standard (ANSI/EIA-748). ## 🔍 Methods of Project Evaluation — The Multi-Dimensional View ### 1. Financial Evaluation (Commercial Viability) Uses discounted cash flow techniques: - **Net Present Value (NPV)** → $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** → The discount rate $r$ that makes $NPV = 0$. - **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** → $BCR = \dfrac{\sum PV\ of\ Benefits}{\sum PV\ of\ Costs}$ - **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** → Time for cumulative cash inflows to equal cumulative outflows. > *Decision Rule:* Accept project if $NPV \geq 0$, $IRR \geq$ hurdle rate, $BCR \geq 1$. ### 2. Economic Evaluation (National Welfare / Social Welfare) Evaluates projects from the **nation's perspective** using: - **Shadow prices** (instead of market prices — to correct for market distortions). - **Social discount rate** (often 8–12% in India, lower than commercial rate). - **Conversion factors** to translate private costs into economic costs. - Considers **externalities** (positive and negative spill-overs). ### 3. Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (SCBA) Quantifies social impact using: - **Social Opportunity Cost of Capital (SOCC).** - **Adjusted benefits** — includes employment generation, equity, health, education. - **Distribution weights** — gives more weight to benefits accruing to the poor (Dalton-Edgworth weights). ### 4. Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Mandated under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986** (India) for 39 categories of projects. Covers: - Air, water, soil, noise, biodiversity impact. - Mitigation measures. - Risk assessment of hazardous materials. ### 5. Project Audit A structured, independent review of project management processes. Two types: - **Internal Audit** — by the organization's own audit cell. - **External Audit** — by an independent third party (e.g., CAG for government projects). # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧪 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — EVM Mastery > **Problem (Model Question, 14 Marks):** > A construction project has the following data at the **end of Month 5**: > - **Budget at Completion (BAC)** = ₹10,00,000 > - **Planned Value (PV)** at Month 5 = ₹5,00,000 > - **Earned Value (EV)** at Month 5 = ₹4,00,000 > - **Actual Cost (AC)** at Month 5 = ₹4,50,000 > > **Required:** > (a) Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI. Interpret the project's status. (7 marks) > (b) Calculate EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Advise the project manager. (7 marks) ### Part (a) Solution — Status Diagnosis **Step 1: Cost Variance (CV)** $$CV = EV - AC$$ $$CV = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = -50{,}000$$ **Interpretation logic:** A *negative* CV means the project has spent **more** money than the value of work it has produced. The project is currently **over budget by ₹50,000**. **Step 2: Schedule Variance (SV)** $$SV = EV - PV$$ $$SV = 4{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000$$ **Interpretation logic:** A *negative* SV means the work performed (₹4,00,000 worth) is **less** than the work scheduled (₹5,00,000 worth). The project is **behind schedule by ₹1,00,000 worth of work**. **Step 3: Cost Performance Index (CPI)** $$CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = 0.8889$$ **Interpretation logic:** $CPI < 1$ means every **₹1 spent is producing only ₹0.89 of work**. The project is over-spending relative to its budget efficiency. **Step 4: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** $$SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.80$$ **Interpretation logic:** $SPI < 1$ means the project is progressing at **80% of the planned rate**. It needs to accelerate. **Summary Status (this is the examiner's favourite tabular conclusion):** | Index | Value | Status | |---|---|---| | CV | −50,000 | Over Budget ❌ | | SV | −1,00,000 | Behind Schedule ❌ | | CPI | 0.89 | Cost Inefficiency ❌ | | SPI | 0.80 | Schedule Lag ❌ | > **Verdict:** The project is in a **"Critical Quadruple"** — over budget *and* behind schedule. Recovery action is mandatory. --- ### Part (b) Solution — Forecasting **Step 5: Estimate at Completion (EAC)** We use the standard formula (assuming current CPI continues): $$EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.8889}$$ $$EAC = 12{,}50{,}000\ (approx)$$ **Interpretation logic:** If the project's poor cost-efficiency continues, the **final total cost** will be **₹12,50,000** instead of the budgeted **₹10,00,000** — a **25% cost overrun**. **Step 6: Estimate to Complete (ETC)** $$ETC = EAC - AC = 12{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000$$ **Interpretation logic:** Approximately **₹8,00,000** of additional money is needed to complete the remaining work. **Step 7: Variance at Completion (VAC)** $$VAC = BAC - EAC = 10{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}50{,}000 = -2{,}50{,}000$$ **Interpretation logic:** A *negative* VAC means the project is **expected to end with a ₹2,50,000 overrun**. **Step 8: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** $$TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{EAC - AC} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{12{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000}$$ $$TCPI = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.75$$ **Interpretation logic:** To meet the original BAC (i.e., to recover the overrun), the project manager must drive the **remaining work at 0.75 efficiency** — which is **worse** than the current CPI of 0.89. **This is mathematically infeasible** because you cannot perform the remaining work at a *lower* efficiency than the current one. **Conclusion:** The BAC is unrecoverable; the manager must **renegotiate the budget** or **reduce scope**. ## 🧭 Decision Matrix — "What Action to Take?" | TCPI vs. CPI | Interpretation | Recommended Action | |---|---|---| | $TCPI > CPI$ | Aggressive target, but possible | Push for cost-control measures | | $TCPI = CPI$ | Maintain current performance | Continue as-is with monitoring | | $TCPI < CPI$ | BAC is unrecoverable | Renegotiate BAC, reduce scope, or crash schedule | > [!NOTE] > **The TCPI Rule of Thumb:** If $TCPI > 1$, the recovery is *very difficult*; if $TCPI > 1.10$, it is *almost impossible*. Always present this in the conclusion for full marks. ## 📐 Derivation of TCPI Logic (Conceptual) The reasoning is grounded in a simple algebraic identity: $$BAC = EV + (BAC - EV)$$ The *unfinished* budget is $(BAC - EV)$. To complete the project at the *target* final cost of $EAC$, the *unfinished cost* must equal $(EAC - AC)$. Therefore, the **cost efficiency required for the remaining work** is: $$TCPI = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Work Value}}{\text{Remaining Money Available}} = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{EAC - AC}$$ This derivation is the foundation of every EVM forecasting answer. # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🗺️ Diagram 1 — The Project Evaluation Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Project Kickoff] --> B[Ex-ante Evaluation
Feasibility Check] B --> C[Project Execution Phase] C --> D[Mid-term Evaluation
Concurrent Review] D --> E[Mid-term Corrective Actions] E --> C C --> F[Project Closure] F --> G[Terminal Evaluation
Final Performance Review] G --> H[Ex-post Evaluation
1 to 3 Years Later
Impact Assessment] H --> I[Lessons Learned Database] I --> J[Strategic Feedback to Organization] J --> K[Future Project Planning] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 🗺️ Diagram 2 — The EVM Performance Matrix (Status Grid) ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Quadrant 1
SV Positive, CV Positive
AHEAD and UNDER BUDGET
Status: Excellent] Q2[Quadrant 2
SV Negative, CV Positive
BEHIND but UNDER BUDGET
Status: Recoverable] Q3[Quadrant 3
SV Negative, CV Negative
BEHIND and OVER BUDGET
Status: Critical] Q4[Quadrant 4
SV Positive, CV Negative
AHEAD but OVER BUDGET
Status: Watch Carefully] Q1 --- Q2 Q2 --- Q3 Q3 --- Q4 Q4 --- Q1 style Q1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style Q2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style Q3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px style Q4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quadrant 3** is the *project manager's nightmare* — both SV and CV are negative. The example worked out in Section 3 fell in **Quadrant 3**. Always label the quadrant in your EVM answers. ## 🗺️ Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture: Project Evaluation System ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[INPUT LAYER
Project Charter, Plans, Baselines,
Actual Data, MIS Reports] --> PROC subgraph PROC[PROCESSING LAYER] V[Variance Analysis Engine] E[Earned Value Calculator] T[Trend and Forecast Engine] P[Performance Index Generator] end INPUT --> V INPUT --> E INPUT --> T V --> OUTPUT E --> OUTPUT T --> OUTPUT P --> OUTPUT subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER] R1[Performance Reports] R2[Variance Dashboards] R3[Forecast Estimates EAC ETC VAC] R4[Corrective Action Recommendations] R5[Lessons Learned Repository] end OUTPUT --> FEEDBACK[Feedback to PM and Steering Committee] style INPUT fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style PROC fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style OUTPUT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style FEEDBACK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 🗺️ Diagram 4 — Methods of Project Evaluation Tree ```mermaid mindmap root((Project Evaluation Methods)) Financial NPV IRR BCR Pay Back Period Economic Shadow Pricing Social Discount Rate Conversion Factors Social Employment Generation Equity Impact Health Education Outcomes Environmental EIA Carbon Footprint Biodiversity Index Technical Performance Testing Reliability Metrics Quality Defect Density Managerial Earned Value Analysis SWOT Project Audit ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** **Define project evaluation. List any four types of project evaluation.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > Project evaluation is the systematic and objective assessment of a project's design, implementation, and results to determine its **relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability**. It provides feedback for control, accountability, and organizational learning. > > **Four Types:** > 1. **Ex-ante evaluation** — conducted *before* the project begins (feasibility check). > 2. **Mid-term / Concurrent evaluation** — conducted *during* execution to enable mid-course correction. > 3. **Terminal evaluation** — conducted *at the end* of the project to assess final outcomes. > 4. **Ex-post evaluation** — conducted *1–3 years after* completion to assess long-term impact and sustainability. ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]** **Distinguish between *formative* and *summative* evaluation.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Formative Evaluation | Summative Evaluation | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | To *improve* the project while it is in progress | To *judge* the project at its end | | **Timing** | Periodic, during execution | At completion (terminal) | | **Audience** | Project management team | Stakeholders, sponsors, public | | **Focus** | Process and mid-course correction | End-results and outcomes | | **Nature** | Diagnostic, iterative | Final, conclusive | --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **Module 4 Internal Choice:** *Answer any ONE of the following.* --- ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Explain the **Earned Value Management (EVM)** system. With the help of neat diagrams, describe the **four cost-schedule quadrants** that classify project performance. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A project has **BAC = ₹20,00,000**, **PV = ₹8,00,000**, **EV = ₹6,00,000**, and **AC = ₹7,00,000** at the end of Month 4. Calculate **CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI**. Interpret the results and advise the project manager. *(7 marks)* #### Part (a) Model Answer **Earned Value Management (EVM)** is an integrated project management technique that measures project performance by combining **scope, schedule, and cost** into a single framework. It uses three baseline values: - **Planned Value (PV)** — the budgeted cost of work *scheduled*. - **Earned Value (EV)** — the budgeted cost of work *actually performed*. - **Actual Cost (AC)** — the actual money spent on work performed. From these, four indices are derived: **CV, SV, CPI, SPI**. **The Four Quadrants (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4):** 1. **Quadrant I (SV > 0, CV > 0):** Project is **ahead of schedule and under budget** — *Excellent*. 2. **Quadrant II (SV < 0, CV > 0):** Project is **behind schedule but under budget** — *Recoverable*; the project can crash or fast-track. 3. **Quadrant III (SV < 0, CV < 0):** Project is **behind schedule and over budget** — *Critical*; severe corrective action needed. 4. **Quadrant IV (SV > 0, CV < 0):** Project is **ahead of schedule but over budget** — *Watch carefully*; the team may be crashing without proper cost control. > **EVM utility:** It enables early detection of cost and schedule deviations, facilitates forecasting, and supports evidence-based decision-making. #### Part (b) Model Answer — Step-by-Step **Step 1: CV** $$CV = EV - AC = 6{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000$$ → Over budget by ₹1,00,000. **Step 2: SV** $$SV = EV - PV = 6{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000$$ → Behind schedule by ₹2,00,000 worth of work. **Step 3: CPI** $$CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 0.857$$ → Every ₹1 spent produces only ₹0.857 of work. **Step 4: SPI** $$SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.75$$ → Project is progressing at 75% of planned rate. **Step 5: EAC** $$EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.857} = 23{,}33{,}333$$ → Expected final cost ₹23,33,333. **Step 6: ETC** $$ETC = EAC - AC = 23{,}33{,}333 - 7{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}33{,}333$$ → Approximately ₹16,33,333 still required. **Step 7: VAC** $$VAC = BAC - EAC = 20{,}00{,}000 - 23{,}33{,}333 = -3{,}33{,}333$$ → Expected cost overrun of ₹3,33,333. **Step 8: TCPI** $$TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{EAC - AC} = \dfrac{14{,}00{,}000}{16{,}33{,}333} = 0.857$$ → $TCPI = 0.857$, which is **less than current CPI (0.857)**. This means the **BAC is technically recoverable** at the current efficiency — but with **no margin for further slippage**. **Advice:** The project is in **Quadrant III (Critical)**. The manager must (i) crash the schedule by adding resources, (ii) tighten cost controls, and (iii) escalate to the steering committee for early-warning action. --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]** **(a)** Discuss the **methods of project evaluation** with reference to **financial, economic, social, and environmental** evaluation. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the concept of **Project Audit**. Differentiate between **internal** and **external** project audits. What are the **steps in conducting a project audit**? *(7 marks)* #### Part (a) Model Answer **Methods of project evaluation** are categorized by the *dimension* of impact being assessed: 1. **Financial Evaluation** — Assesses the project's commercial viability using discounted cash flow techniques: **NPV, IRR, BCR, and Pay-Back Period**. Decision criteria: $NPV \geq 0$, $IRR \geq$ hurdle rate, $BCR \geq 1$. 2. **Economic Evaluation** — Assesses the project from the **nation's perspective** by correcting market prices for distortions. Uses **shadow prices**, **social discount rate** (lower than market rate), and **conversion factors**. Captures **externalities** not reflected in market transactions. 3. **Social Evaluation (SCBA)** — Quantifies social impact using **Social Opportunity Cost of Capital (SOCC)** and **distribution weights** (favoring benefits to the poor). Considers employment generation, equity, health, education, and gender impact. 4. **Environmental Evaluation (EIA)** — Mandated under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986** for 39 project categories. Evaluates impact on air, water, soil, noise, and biodiversity; prescribes mitigation measures. > A *comprehensive* project evaluation must integrate all four dimensions to ensure the project is **commercially viable, economically efficient, socially just, and environmentally sustainable** — the four pillars of sustainable development. #### Part (b) Model Answer **Project Audit** is a *structured, independent examination* of a project's management processes, performance, and outcomes. It verifies that the project has followed approved plans, complied with standards, and delivered expected value. **Difference between Internal and External Audit:** | Basis | Internal Audit | External Audit | |---|---|---| | **Conducted by** | The organization's own audit cell | An independent third party (e.g., CAG) | | **Objectivity** | Partial (insider view) | High (outsider view) | | **Scope** | Process compliance, internal controls | Statutory compliance, public accountability | | **Frequency** | Periodic | At closure or on demand | | **Reporting** | To top management | To sponsors, regulators, public | **Steps in Conducting a Project Audit:** 1. **Define audit scope and objectives** — what is being audited and why. 2. **Develop audit plan and checklist** — based on approved baselines. 3. **Gather data** — through document review, interviews, site visits. 4. **Evaluate against criteria** — planned vs. actual, compliance vs. standards. 5. **Identify variances, non-conformities, and risks.** 6. **Prepare audit report** — findings, conclusions, recommendations. 7. **Present to management and follow up** — closure of audit observations. > A well-conducted project audit **institutionalizes learning** and prevents the recurrence of past mistakes. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Mixing up the formulas for CV and SV** — *Always* remember: CV uses **AC**, SV uses **PV**. The mnemonic is *"**C**ost is **C**ompared with **A**ctual" and *"**S**chedule is **S**een against **P**lan."* > 2. **Forgetting units** — Always state *currency* (₹) and *time period* (Month X). > 3. **Not interpreting indices** — Simply computing CPI = 0.85 is *insufficient*; you **must** state *"the project is over-spending, getting only ₹0.85 of work for every ₹1 spent."* > 4. **Ignoring the EAC formula variant** — KTU may ask for EAC under *different assumptions* (e.g., *"if future performance matches planned"* → $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$). Know all variants. > 5. **Writing the definition of evaluation as a one-liner** — Examiners award 1 mark *separately* for mentioning *"continuous," "systematic," "multi-dimensional,"* and *"feedback."* > 6. **Conflating formative with concurrent evaluation** — Formative is *iterative improvement*; concurrent is *mid-term review.* They are not identical. > 7. **Writing the EAC formula without specifying the assumption** — Always state *"assuming current cost performance continues."* --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 12-Point High-Density Recap for Module 4 — Project Evaluation** - ✅ **Definition** — Systematic, objective, *continuous*, multi-dimensional assessment of project performance for relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability. - ✅ **Four Pillars (R-E-E-I)** — **R**elevance, **E**ffectiveness, **E**fficiency, **I**mpact. - ✅ **Four Timing Types** — Ex-ante, Concurrent (Mid-term), Terminal, Ex-post. - ✅ **Two Purpose Types** — Formative (improve) vs. Summative (judge). - ✅ **EVM Triad** — **PV** (planned), **EV** (earned), **AC** (actual). - ✅ **Four EVM Indices** — $CV = EV - AC$, $SV = EV - PV$, $CPI = EV / AC$, $SPI = EV / PV$. - ✅ **Four Forecast Indices** — $EAC = BAC / CPI$, $ETC = EAC - AC$, $VAC = BAC - EAC$, $TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (EAC - AC)$. - ✅ **Quadrant Rule** — *Both positive* = excellent; *both negative* = critical. - ✅ **NPV Rule** — Accept if $NPV \geq 0$. - ✅ **IRR Rule** — Accept if $IRR \geq$ hurdle rate (cost of capital). - ✅ **BCR Rule** — Accept if $BCR \geq 1$. - ✅ **Project Audit** — Independent review; Internal vs. External. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Mantra for Module 4:** *"Define with R-E-E-I. Diagnose with EVM. Decide with the quadrant. Defend your conclusion with TCPI."*

topics.title: Project Progress and Performance Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Progress and Performance Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Progress and Performance Management (PPPM)** is the systematic, continuous process of **measuring, monitoring, analyzing, and controlling** the actual work accomplished against the planned schedule and the actual cost incurred against the planned budget, using standardized metrics such as **Earned Value Management (EVM)**, **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**, and **S-Curve Analysis**, to enable data-driven corrective action and ensure successful project completion within the **Triple Constraint** (Scope, Time, Cost).

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the *PMBOK Guide* and *PRINCE2* frameworks, Project Progress and Performance Management is the **Monitoring & Controlling Process Group's** central activity. It transforms raw project data into **management intelligence** through the three pillars:

1. **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** — The approved, time-phased budget used as the reference.
2. **Earned Value Analysis** — Integrating scope, schedule, and cost to answer the question: *"What did we get for what we spent?"*
3. **Variance Analysis & Forecasting** — Comparing actuals against the baseline to predict future performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Driving a Car on a Highway Trip**
>
> Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** (a 2,500 km journey) in a car. The trip plan says you should be at **Nagpur (1,500 km)** by 6:00 PM today.
>
> - **Planned Value (PV)** = The odometer reading you *should* have at this moment (1,500 km).
> - **Earned Value (EV)** = The odometer reading you have *actually* achieved (say, 1,200 km at Bhopal).
> - **Actual Cost (AC)** = The money (fuel + tolls) you have *actually* spent so far.
> - **Schedule Variance (SV = EV − PV)** = You are behind by 300 km. The dashboard shows a "behind schedule" warning.
> - **Cost Variance (CV = EV − AC)** = If you spent ₹8,000 for 1,200 km instead of the budgeted ₹6,000, you have a cost overrun.
> - **SPI & CPI** = Two gauges on your dashboard telling the driver-manager exactly *how* off-track they are.
> - **EAC** = Your GPS's predicted total fuel cost when you finally reach Delhi.
>
> Just as a driver cannot safely reach a destination without dashboard feedback, a project manager cannot deliver a project without continuous **Progress and Performance Management** feedback loops.

### 1.3 The Triple Constraint Triangle

The three dimensions simultaneously measured in PPPM are:

- **Scope** — *What* work is done (measured in % complete or deliverables).
- **Schedule** — *When* work is done (measured against planned dates).
- **Cost** — *How much* is spent (measured against budgeted cost).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Progress and Performance Management is **not** the same as "Project Closing." It is a **continuous, recurring activity** that runs parallel to execution — feeding data into the **Reporting Cycle** so that the Project Manager can issue *Corrective Actions*, *Preventive Actions*, and *Defect Repairs*.

### 1.4 Key Performance Concepts at a Glance

| # | Concept | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** | Hierarchical decomposition of total project scope. |
| 2 | **Control Accounts (CA)** | Management control points within the WBS. |
| 3 | **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** | Approved time-phased budget plan. |
| 4 | **Planned Value (PV)** | Authorized budget for scheduled work. |
| 5 | **Earned Value (EV)** | Authorized budget for actually completed work. |
| 6 | **Actual Cost (AC)** | Real cost incurred for completed work. |
| 7 | **Cost Variance (CV)** | Difference indicating budget efficiency. |
| 8 | **Schedule Variance (SV)** | Difference indicating schedule efficiency. |
| 9 | **CPI / SPI Indices** | Ratio-based efficiency indicators. |
| 10 | **EAC / ETC / VAC** | Forecasted completion metrics. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 5-Step Progress Management Logic

The execution of Project Progress and Performance Management follows a structured, cyclical **5-step logical sequence**:

**Step 1 — Establish the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)**
- Sum the time-phased budgets of all **Work Packages** approved in the project plan.
- This becomes the **cost-time graph** (S-Curve) against which all actuals are measured.
- *Why?* Without a baseline, deviation cannot be quantified.

**Step 2 — Measure Physical Progress (% Complete)**
- Each **Work Package** is measured using one of three industry-standard techniques:
  - **0/100 Rule** — Activity is 0% complete until finished, then 100% (used for discrete deliverables).
  - **50/50 Rule** — 50% credit at start, 50% at finish (for short-duration tasks).
  - **Equivalent Units / Weighted Milestone** — Pro-rata credit based on milestones achieved.
- *Why?* Earned Value requires a defensible measure of "how much" was done.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Three Core EVM Values (PV, EV, AC)**
- **PV** is *read* from the baseline at the current status date.
- **EV** is *calculated* as: $\text{EV} = \text{% Complete} \times \text{BAC of Work Package}$.
- **AC** is *collected* from the cost ledger / timesheets / invoices.
- *Why?* These three numbers are the *only* raw inputs needed for the entire EVM system.

**Step 4 — Compute Variances and Performance Indices**
- Variances (CV, SV) show *absolute* deviation in currency terms.
- Indices (CPI, SPI) show *relative* efficiency as dimensionless ratios.
- *Why?* Indices are unit-free and allow comparison across projects of any size.

**Step 5 — Forecast, Report, and Trigger Corrective Action**
- Use **EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI** to predict the final outcome.
- If CPI < 1.0 or SPI < 1.0, issue a **Change Request** or **Corrective Action**.
- Re-baseline only through formal **Integrated Change Control**.
- *Why?* Forecasting allows proactive steering rather than reactive crisis management.

### 2.2 The Eight EVM Variables — Detailed Description

| Variable | Full Name | Meaning | Notation Tip |
|----------|-----------|---------|--------------|
| **PV** | Planned Value (BCWS) | Budgeted cost of work *scheduled*. | Read from baseline. |
| **EV** | Earned Value (BCWP) | Budgeted cost of work *performed*. | Calculated. |
| **AC** | Actual Cost (ACWP) | Real cost of work *performed*. | Collected. |
| **BAC** | Budget at Completion | Total budget approved for the project. | Fixed at approval. |
| **CV** | Cost Variance | EV − AC. Negative = Over Budget. | Currency. |
| **SV** | Schedule Variance | EV − PV. Negative = Behind Schedule. | Currency. |
| **EAC** | Estimate at Completion | Forecasted total project cost. | Updated each period. |
| **VAC** | Variance at Completion | BAC − EAC. | Currency. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield EVM Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CARD — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**
> The equations below are universally valid for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numerical questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Schedule Variance (SV)} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\[4pt]
\text{Cost Variance (CV)} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\[4pt]
\text{Schedule Performance Index (SPI)} &= \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\[4pt]
\text{Cost Performance Index (CPI)} &= \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\[4pt]
\text{Estimate at Completion (EAC)} &= \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\[4pt]
\text{Estimate to Complete (ETC)} &= \text{EAC} - \text{AC} \\[4pt]
\text{Variance at Completion (VAC)} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\[4pt]
\text{To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)} &= \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} \\[4pt]
\text{Percent Complete (PC)} &= \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{BAC}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Variance Analysis — Interpreting the Numbers

| Metric | Value Range | Interpretation | Managerial Action |
|--------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **SPI = 1.0** | Exactly on schedule | Project is proceeding exactly as planned. | Continue monitoring. |
| **SPI > 1.0** | Ahead of schedule | Work completed is more than planned. | Validate with quality checks. |
| **SPI < 1.0** | Behind schedule | Less work done than planned. | Crashing / Fast-tracking. |
| **CPI = 1.0** | Exactly on budget | Cost matches value earned. | Continue. |
| **CPI > 1.0** | Under budget | Cost efficiency is good. | Document best practices. |
| **CPI < 1.0** | Over budget | Spending more than value earned. | Cost reduction / scope review. |

### 2.5 Performance Reporting Cycle (KTU Favourite Topic)

The performance reporting follows a **PDCA-style closed loop**:

> **Plan (Baseline)** → **Execute (Actual Work)** → **Measure (Collect AC & % Complete)** → **Analyze (Compute EV, CV, SV, CPI, SPI)** → **Report (S-Curves, Dashboards)** → **Act (Corrective Action / Re-plan)**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Projects (Civil):** EVM is mandated by most EPC contracts in Kerala (KMRL, GIFT projects).
- **Software Industry:** Agile teams use **Burn-Down Charts** — a direct cousin of EVM's S-Curve.
- **Defence Projects (DRDO):** Strict EVM compliance under *Make-in-India* procurement guidelines.
- **Manufacturing:** Used for cost control in mass production lines and Six Sigma DMAIC cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In the absence of EVM, a manager only sees *spent money*. With EVM, the manager sees *value created per rupee spent* — a fundamentally superior decision-making signal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — EVM Index Formulas

We will derive the four foundational indices from first principles so the student understands the *logic*, not just the memorization.

**Derivation of SPI (Schedule Performance Index)**

> **Step 1 — Define the concept.**
> Schedule efficiency is the *ratio* of the value of work actually completed to the value of work that *should* have been completed by this date.

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{Value of Work Actually Done}}{\text{Value of Work Planned to be Done}}
$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute the EVM terms.**
> The "value of work actually done" is **EV** (Earned Value). The "value of work planned" is **PV** (Planned Value).

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}
$$

> **Step 3 — Sanity check.**
> - If EV = PV, then SPI = 1.0 (perfectly on schedule).
> - If EV < PV, then SPI < 1.0 (behind schedule — numerator is smaller).
> - If EV > PV, then SPI > 1.0 (ahead of schedule — numerator is larger).
> The dimensionless ratio is project-size independent. $\blacksquare$

**Derivation of CPI (Cost Performance Index)**

> **Step 1 — Define cost efficiency as a ratio.**
> Cost efficiency is the value earned per unit of money actually spent.

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Value Earned}}{\text{Money Actually Spent}}
$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute EVM terms.**
> Value earned = **EV**; Money spent = **AC**.

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}
$$

> **Step 3 — Interpretation logic.**
> - CPI = 1.0 → every ₹1 spent produced ₹1 of value.
> - CPI = 0.80 → every ₹1 spent produced only ₹0.80 of value (20% inefficient).
> - CPI = 1.25 → every ₹1 spent produced ₹1.25 of value (25% efficient). $\blacksquare$

**Derivation of EAC (Estimate at Completion)**

> **Step 1 — Establish assumption.**
> We assume the *past cost performance* (CPI) will continue for the remaining work. This is the most common KTU-board assumption.

$$
\text{Expected Final Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Total Budget}}{\text{Cost Efficiency so far}}
$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute the variables.**

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}
$$

> **Step 3 — Cross-check using the identity: BAC = AC + ETC.**
> Given ETC = EAC − AC, the relationship is internally consistent. $\blacksquare$

**Derivation of TCPI (To-Complete Performance Index)**

> **Step 1 — Define the remaining work.**
> Remaining work in **cost** = BAC − EV. Remaining work in **money** = BAC − AC.

> **Step 2 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\text{TCPI} = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Work Value}}{\text{Remaining Budget}} = \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}
$$

> **Step 3 — Interpretation.**
> TCPI > 1.0 means the project team must *improve* efficiency to meet BAC. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style (14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Pattern):**
> A project has a **Budget at Completion (BAC) of ₹50,00,000**. As on the status date:
> - **Planned Value (PV) = ₹20,00,000**
> - **Actual Cost (AC) = ₹22,00,000**
> - **Earned Value (EV) = ₹18,00,000**
>
> Compute: SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI, and the % Complete. Interpret the project status.

**Solution — Step-by-Step (Full Valuation-Ready):**

**Step A — Schedule Variance (SV)**

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -\,2{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* Negative SV ⇒ the project is **behind schedule by ₹2,00,000 worth of work**.

**Step B — Cost Variance (CV)**

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000 = -\,4{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* Negative CV ⇒ the project is **over budget by ₹4,00,000**.

**Step C — Schedule Performance Index (SPI)**

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.90
$$

*Interpretation:* For every **1 hour of work planned**, only **0.90 hours of work** have been earned. The project is progressing at **90% of the planned rate**.

**Step D — Cost Performance Index (CPI)**

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.818
$$

*Interpretation:* For every **₹1 spent**, only **₹0.818 of value** has been earned. The project is **~18.2% cost-inefficient**.

**Step E — Estimate at Completion (EAC)**

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.818} \approx 61{,}12{,}500
$$

*Interpretation:* If the current cost performance continues, the project will **overshoot the budget by ₹11,12,500** and finish at approximately **₹61.13 Lakhs**.

**Step F — Estimate to Complete (ETC)**

$$
\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC} = 61{,}12{,}500 - 22{,}00{,}000 = 39{,}12{,}500
$$

*Interpretation:* Approximately **₹39.13 Lakhs more** will be required to finish the remaining work.

**Step G — Variance at Completion (VAC)**

$$
