\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 61{,}12{,}500 = -\,11{,}12{,}500
$$

*Interpretation:* At completion, the project is forecasted to be **over budget by ₹11.13 Lakhs**.

**Step H — To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)**

$$
\text{TCPI} = \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{32{,}00{,}000}{28{,}00{,}000} \approx 1.143
$$

*Interpretation:* To finish the project **on the original BAC of ₹50L**, the remaining work must be performed at a CPI of **1.143** — i.e., the team must become **14.3% more cost-efficient** than originally planned.

**Step I — Percent Complete**

$$
\%\text{ Complete} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{BAC}} \times 100 = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 36\%
$$

*Interpretation:* The project is **36% complete in terms of value earned**, but the time elapsed corresponds to PV/BAC = 40% planned completion. This **4% schedule slip** confirms the SPI < 1.0 result.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — KTU Management Interpretation Table

| Index | Computed Value | Status | Recommended Managerial Action |
|-------|----------------|--------|-------------------------------|
| **SPI** | 0.90 | Behind | Apply **Crashing** (add resources) or **Fast-Tracking** (parallel tasks). |
| **CPI** | 0.818 | Over Budget | Renegotiate vendor contracts; conduct **Value Engineering**. |
| **TCPI** | 1.143 | Unfavourable | Tighten cost controls; reduce scope if TCPI > 1.2. |
| **VAC** | −₹11.13 L | Loss | Trigger **Integrated Change Control**; inform sponsor. |
| **% Complete** | 36% | Lagging | Re-baseline the schedule only with formal change approval. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The EVM Data Flow — From Data to Decision

The following block diagram illustrates how raw project data is transformed into managerial decisions through the EVM system. Each block represents a transformation step; each arrow represents a data dependency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Project Plan and WBS] --> nodeB[Performance Measurement Baseline PMB]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Time Phased PV Curve]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Status Date Snapshot]
    nodeE[Actual Work Performed] --> nodeF[Percent Complete Measurement]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute EV]
    nodeH[Cost Ledger and Invoices] --> nodeI[Collect AC]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Variance Analysis CV and SV]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Performance Indices CPI and SPI]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Forecasting EAC ETC VAC TCPI]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Performance Reports and S Curves]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Corrective and Preventive Actions]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Integrated Change Control]
    nodeO --> nodeB
```

### 4.2 The Performance Reporting Cycle (PDCA Loop)

This diagram shows the cyclical nature of progress and performance management as a closed-loop control system. Note the explicit feedback arrow from "Corrective Action" back to the baseline, symbolizing the re-planning that follows change control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Plan - Establish PMB] --> nodeQ[Do - Execute Work Packages]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Check - Measure EV and AC]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Act - Variance Analysis and Forecasting]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Decide - Corrective Action YES or NO]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Re Plan - Update Baseline via Change Control]
    nodeU --> nodeP
    nodeS --> nodeV[Report - Performance Dashboard and S Curve]
```

### 4.3 The S-Curve Visualization (Block Architecture)

Since an actual graphical S-curve requires coordinate plotting, the following block architecture represents the **three stacked curves** that constitute a standard Earned Value S-Curve chart. Each block represents a curve; the arrows represent their relative positions at the status date.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX[Top Curve - PV BCWS Planned Value] --> nodeY[Middle Curve - EV BCWP Earned Value]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Bottom Curve - AC ACWP Actual Cost]
    nodeX -. EV is below PV means behind schedule .-> nodeY
    nodeY -. EV is above AC means over budget .-> nodeZ
    nodeA1[Status Date Vertical Line] --> nodeX
    nodeA1 --> nodeY
    nodeA1 --> nodeZ
    nodeB1[Schedule Gap between PV and EV at status date equals SV] --> nodeX
    nodeB1 --> nodeY
    nodeC1[Cost Gap between EV and AC at status date equals CV] --> nodeY
    nodeC1 --> nodeZ
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Performance Measurement (WBS to Activity)

This block diagram depicts how progress data cascades from the highest level of the **Work Breakdown Structure** down to the lowest **Activity Level**, then aggregates upward to support the EVM calculations at the Control Account level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeW1[Project Level Total BAC] --> nodeW2[Control Account 1]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW3[Work Package 1A]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW4[Work Package 1B]
    nodeW3 --> nodeW5[Activity 1A1]
    nodeW3 --> nodeW6[Activity 1A2]
    nodeW4 --> nodeW7[Activity 1B1]
    nodeW4 --> nodeW8[Activity 1B2]
    nodeW5 --> nodeM1[Collect AC and Percent Complete]
    nodeW6 --> nodeM1
    nodeW7 --> nodeM1
    nodeW8 --> nodeM1
    nodeM1 --> nodeM2[Compute EV at Work Package Level]
    nodeM2 --> nodeM3[Roll up to Control Account EV]
    nodeM3 --> nodeM4[Roll up to Project Level EV]
```

### 4.5 The Decision Tree — Interpreting CPI and SPI

This decision tree provides a binary interpretation logic. The PM enters the tree with two ratio values and exits with one of four managerial conclusions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeI1[Compute CPI and SPI] --> nodeI2{CPI greater than or equal to 1.0}
    nodeI2 -- YES --> nodeI3{On Budget}
    nodeI2 -- NO --> nodeI4{Cost Overrun}
    nodeI3 --> nodeI5{SPI greater than or equal to 1.0}
    nodeI5 -- YES --> nodeI6[Status ON TRACK - Maintain Plan]
    nodeI5 -- NO --> nodeI7[Status BEHIND but within budget - Crashing needed]
    nodeI4 --> nodeI8{SPI greater than or equal to 1.0}
    nodeI8 -- YES --> nodeI9[Status AHEAD of schedule but over budget - Cost control needed]
    nodeI8 -- NO --> nodeI10[Status CRITICAL - Behind schedule and over budget - Re plan and recover]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> **Define Earned Value Management (EVM). List any four key EVM metrics.**

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**
> Earned Value Management is a project performance measurement technique that integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** to assess project progress objectively. It compares the *planned value* of work, the *earned value* of work actually completed, and the *actual cost* incurred, enabling variance analysis and forecasting.
>
> Four key EVM metrics are:
> 1. **Planned Value (PV)** — Budgeted cost of work scheduled.
> 2. **Earned Value (EV)** — Budgeted cost of work performed.
> 3. **Actual Cost (AC)** — Real cost incurred.
> 4. **Cost Performance Index (CPI) = EV / AC.**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Differentiate between Estimate at Completion (EAC) and Estimate to Complete (ETC).**

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**

| Aspect | EAC | ETC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Definition** | Forecasted *total* project cost at completion. | Forecasted *remaining* cost to finish the project. |
| **Formula** | EAC = BAC / CPI | ETC = EAC − AC |
| **Scope** | Includes work already done. | Refers only to the remaining work. |
| **Use** | Used to predict the *final overrun* or *savings*. | Used to plan the *remaining budget* requirement. |
| **Decision** | Helps the sponsor decide on additional funding. | Helps the PM plan resource allocation. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **SV (Schedule Variance) with SPI (Schedule Performance Index)**. SV is in **rupees**; SPI is a **ratio**.
> - Many students forget to **substitute CPI in the denominator** when computing EAC. KTU examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Always** interpret the result (one sentence) — KTU awards a *separate* "interpretation" mark.
> - For TCPI, do **not** write (BAC − EV) / AC. The correct denominator is **BAC − AC**.

---

#### **Part B — Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Earned Value Management (EVM)**. With a neat sketch, describe the **EVM S-Curve** showing PV, EV, and AC. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A software project has BAC = **₹40,00,000**. At the end of Month 4, the project manager reports: PV = **₹18,00,000**, AC = **₹20,00,000**, EV = **₹16,00,000**. Calculate SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Interpret each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept and S-Curve (7 Marks):**

EVM is a **scope-schedule-cost integrated** performance measurement methodology. It requires a **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** established at the time of plan approval. As work progresses, three values are tracked:

- **PV (Planned Value)** — What we *planned* to spend by this date.
- **EV (Earned Value)** — What we *earned* (value of work actually done) by this date.
- **AC (Actual Cost)** — What we *actually spent* by this date.

The **S-Curve** plots cumulative cost (Y-axis) against time (X-axis). Typically, **PV forms the top curve**, **EV is in the middle**, and **AC is at the bottom** when the project is over budget and behind schedule. The vertical distance between PV and EV at the status date represents **SV**; the vertical distance between EV and AC represents **CV**. The S-curve derives its name from the characteristic S-shape of cumulative project expenditure.

> *(Note: Since an actual sketch cannot be drawn in plain text, the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes represents the S-Curve's three stacked curves.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: BAC = 40,00,000; PV = 18,00,000; AC = 20,00,000; EV = 16,00,000.

> **Step 1 — SPI**

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \dfrac{16{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.889
$$

*Interpretation:* Project is progressing at ~88.9% of the planned rate. **Behind schedule.**

> **Step 2 — CPI**

$$
\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \dfrac{16{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

*Interpretation:* For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of value is earned. **20% cost-inefficient.**

> **Step 3 — EAC**

$$
\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = 50{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* Total project cost projected to be **₹50,00,000** — an overrun of **₹10,00,000** over the original budget.

> **Step 4 — ETC**

$$
\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* An additional **₹30,00,000** is required to complete the remaining work.

> **Step 5 — VAC**

$$
\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 40{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 = -\,10{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* A forecasted **loss / overrun of ₹10,00,000** at project completion.

> **Step 6 — TCPI**

$$
\text{TCPI} = \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{24{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 1.20
$$

*Interpretation:* To finish within the original BAC, the remaining work must be executed at a **CPI of 1.20** (i.e., 20% more cost-efficient than originally planned). This is a **challenging target** and indicates the need for immediate corrective action.

---

#### **Part B — Question Choice B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** What is the **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)**? Explain the role of **Control Accounts** and **Work Packages** in progress measurement. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A construction project has BAC = **₹1,00,00,000**. After 6 months: PV = **₹40,00,000**, EV = **₹30,00,000**, AC = **₹37,50,000**. Determine SV, CV, % Schedule Elapsed vs. % Work Done, and forecast the **projected completion duration** assuming schedule performance continues. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PMB, Control Accounts, and Work Packages (7 Marks):**

The **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** is the approved, time-phased budget plan against which project performance is measured and controlled. It is created by summing the cost budgets of all work packages over time, producing the **S-Curve baseline**. The PMB is approved during the *Planning Process Group* and any changes to it must go through *Integrated Change Control*.

**Control Accounts (CAs)** are management control points in the WBS. Each CA is a discrete scope-cost-schedule triple that has a single responsible manager. CAs are at the *Work Package level* and above.

**Work Packages (WPs)** are the lowest level of the WBS — they are small enough to be estimated, scheduled, and monitored accurately. Each WP has its own BAC, % complete, AC, and EV, which are aggregated upward to the CA and project levels. Together, CAs and WPs form the **granularity** needed for meaningful progress measurement.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: BAC = 1,00,00,000; PV = 40,00,000; EV = 30,00,00,000; AC = 37,50,000.

> **Step 1 — SV**

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 30{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}00{,}000 = -\,10{,}00{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* Behind schedule by **₹10,00,000 worth of work**.

> **Step 2 — CV**

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 30{,}00{,}000 - 37{,}50{,}000 = -\,7{,}50{,}000
$$

*Interpretation:* Over budget by **₹7,50,000**.

> **Step 3 — % Schedule Elapsed vs. % Work Done**

$$
\%\text{ Schedule Elapsed (planned)} = \dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text{BAC}} \times 100 = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

$$
\%\text{ Work Done (actual)} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{BAC}} \times 100 = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

*Interpretation:* The project *should* be **40% complete** but is only **30% complete** — a **10% schedule slip**.

> **Step 4 — SPI and Projected Completion Duration**

$$
\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 0.75
$$

Let the **original planned duration** be **T** months. The *earned schedule* concept projects:

$$
\text{Projected Duration} = \dfrac{\text{Original Duration}}{\text{SPI}} = \dfrac{T}{0.75} = 1.333\,T
$$

*Interpretation:* If the current schedule performance continues, the project will take **33.3% longer** than originally planned. For instance, if T = 12 months, the projected duration is **16 months** (a 4-month delay).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Bullet Summary):**
> - **Project Progress and Performance Management** is a *continuous* activity — not a one-time event.
> - **EVM** is the gold standard: PV (planned), EV (earned), AC (actual) — these three values drive *everything*.
> - **SV = EV − PV** (in ₹); **SPI = EV / PV** (ratio). SPI < 1.0 ⇒ behind schedule.
> - **CV = EV − AC** (in ₹); **CPI = EV / AC** (ratio). CPI < 1.0 ⇒ over budget.
> - **EAC = BAC / CPI** is the most commonly tested forecasting formula in KTU board exams.
> - **TCPI = (BAC − EV) / (BAC − AC)** — needed only when BAC is fixed and the project must recover.
> - The **% Complete** must be measured using a *defensible* method: 0/100, 50/50, or Weighted Milestone.
> - The **S-Curve** is the visual signature of PPPM — three stacked curves (PV on top, EV middle, AC at the bottom in an over-budget-behind-schedule scenario).
> - **Re-baselining** requires formal *Integrated Change Control*; never silently change the PMB.
> - **Reporting** must follow the PDCA cycle: Plan → Do → Check → Act → Re-plan.
> - **Variance Analysis** triggers **Corrective Action** (fix the problem) or **Preventive Action** (avoid future problems).
> - **Critical Ratio (CR) = CPI × SPI** is a *bonus* high-yield metric — if CR < 1.0, the project needs recovery.
> - **Earned Schedule (ES)** is the modern extension of EVM that converts schedule analysis to *time units* instead of currency — high-value concept for KTU 2024.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Closure and Oversight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Closure and Oversight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Project Closure

**Project Closure** is the formal, structured, and documented process of finalizing all activities, formally accepting project deliverables, completing contractual obligations, releasing project resources, and archiving project documentation to formally terminate the project or project phase. As per the **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** framework, closure is one of the five process groups and is mandatory for every project, regardless of whether the project ends successfully, is terminated early, or is cancelled.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, project closure is treated as a non-negotiable administrative and strategic milestone. It transforms a "live" project into institutional knowledge and formally hands over the outcome to the operations/support team.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Project closure is NOT just a "wrap-up meeting." It is a *systematic process* with legally binding, financial, and documentation implications. Skipping closure = incomplete project lifecycle.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Project Oversight

**Project Oversight** is the continuous, independent, and structured governance mechanism used by sponsors, steering committees, auditors, and regulatory bodies to ensure that a project remains aligned with its approved scope, schedule, budget, quality standards, and strategic objectives. It provides *independent assurance* that the project manager's execution is sound and that risks are being managed.

Oversight operates at a *higher altitude* than day-to-day project management. While the project manager focuses on *doing* the project, oversight focuses on *ensuring the right project is being done right*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The House-Building Analogy (Project Closure):**
Imagine you built a house. Project closure is the day you:
1. Get the **final occupancy certificate** from the municipal engineer (formal acceptance).
2. Settle the **final bill with the contractor** and hand over the keys (contract closure).
3. File the **blueprints, permits, and warranty cards** in a safe (archiving).
4. Sit with the family and discuss: "What went well? What went wrong?" (lessons learned).
5. Officially **close the builder's temporary site office** (resource release).

Without this, you technically have a house, but legally, financially, and administratively — the project is still "open."

**Analogy 2 — The Driving Test Analogy (Project Oversight):**
While *you* drive the car (project manager executing), the *examiner sitting next to you* (oversight body) is constantly noting: Are you going over the speed limit (budget overrun)? Are you staying in the lane (scope creep)? Are signals being used (communication plan)? The examiner doesn't drive — they *assess*. That assessment is oversight.

### 1.4 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Administrative Closure:** The formal documentation and archiving of project records.
- **Contract Closure:** Settlement and closure of all procurement contracts and agreements.
- **Project Audit:** A structured, independent review of project performance and compliance.
- **Lessons Learned Register:** A documented record of insights gained during the project.
- **Final Project Report:** The comprehensive document summarizing project performance.
- **Punch List:** A list of minor incomplete or non-conforming items remaining at project end.
- **Sponsor Acceptance:** Formal sign-off from the project sponsor on deliverables.
- **Variance Threshold:** The acceptable deviation (usually $\pm 10\%$ for cost and schedule) that triggers oversight intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** *Project Closure = End of Execution + Final Acceptance + Documentation + Lessons Learned. Project Oversight = Continuous Governance + Independent Assurance + Compliance Verification.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Project Closure

A KTU-grade treatment of project closure breaks the process into **six structured pillars**. Mastering these is a high-frequency exam topic.

**Pillar 1 — Final Deliverable Acceptance**
- The customer, sponsor, or client formally verifies that all deliverables meet the agreed-upon scope and quality criteria.
- Documented through a **Project Acceptance Form / Sign-off Document**.
- This step triggers the *warranty period* in most engineering contracts.

**Pillar 2 — Contract Closure (Procurement Closure)**
- All vendor and supplier contracts are formally closed.
- Final payments, performance evaluations, and warranty documents are completed.
- Resolution of any pending claims, disputes, or change orders.

**Pillar 3 — Administrative Closure**
- Updating of all final project documents (project management plan, baselines, schedules).
- Archival of all project records — both physical and digital — as per organizational policy.
- Creation of the **Final Project Report**.

**Pillar 4 — Financial Closure**
- Finalization of all financial accounts.
- Payment of all pending invoices, salaries, and statutory dues.
- Reconciliation of budgeted vs. actual cost.
- Final cost audit.

**Pillar 5 — Lessons Learned & Knowledge Transfer**
- A structured **retrospective / post-mortem meeting** is conducted.
- The **Lessons Learned Register** is finalized and shared with the PMO (Project Management Office).
- Knowledge is transferred to the operations team for sustainability of deliverables.

**Pillar 6 — Resource Release & Team Transition**
- Human resources are formally released back to their functional managers.
- Project-specific infrastructure, licenses, and equipment are returned or reassigned.
- Team performance evaluations are completed and submitted to HR.

### 2.2 The Three Layers of Project Oversight

**Layer 1 — Internal Oversight (within the project)**
- Performed by: **Project Sponsor & Project Steering Committee**.
- Frequency: Monthly or milestone-based **Stage-Gate Reviews**.
- Tools: Progress reports, Earned Value Analysis, Risk Registers.

**Layer 2 — Organizational Oversight (PMO Level)**
- Performed by: **Project Management Office (PMO)**.
- Function: Ensures alignment of the project with the organization's strategic portfolio.
- Tools: Portfolio dashboards, compliance audits, methodology adherence checks.

**Layer 3 — Independent / External Oversight**
- Performed by: **External Auditors, Regulatory Bodies, Government Agencies, Independent Review Committees**.
- Function: Independent assurance, statutory compliance, public accountability (especially in government-funded projects under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** which is Kerala Government).

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Closure Step

| Why is it needed? | How is it done? |
|---|---|
| To legally and formally *end* the project | Through a signed **Project Closure Report** |
| To prevent *re-litigation* of closed issues | Through documented **Acceptance Sign-offs** |
| To free up *resources* for new projects | Through a formal **Resource Release Memorandum** |
| To build *organizational memory* | Through a structured **Lessons Learned Workshop** |
| To enable *future estimates* to be accurate | Through archiving historical **Cost & Schedule Data** |
| To comply with *statutory & audit* needs | Through a final **Project Audit & Financial Reconciliation** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Quantitative Oversight Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Although project closure is qualitative, several **performance variance metrics** are frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). Memorize the formulas below.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost Variance (CV)** | $CV = EV - AC$ | $CV \ge 0$ → Under budget; $CV \lt 0$ → Over budget | Oversight trigger |
| **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $SV = EV - PV$ | $SV \ge 0$ → Ahead; $SV \lt 0$ → Behind | Oversight trigger |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $CPI = EV / AC$ | $CPI \ge 1$ → Efficient; $CPI \lt 1$ → Inefficient | Audit metric |
| **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $SPI = EV / PV$ | $SPI \ge 1$ → On time; $SPI \lt 1$ → Delayed | Audit metric |
| **Estimate at Completion (EAC)** | $EAC = BAC / CPI$ | Forecasted total project cost | Closure forecast |
| **Variance at Completion (VAC)** | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Expected cost overrun/savings at end | Closure audit |
| **To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** | $TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC)$ | Required efficiency to finish on budget | Final-stage control |
| **Project Completion Ratio** | $PCR = EV / BAC$ | Percentage of work actually completed | Closure readiness check |

Where: $EV$ = Earned Value, $AC$ = Actual Cost, $PV$ = Planned Value, $BAC$ = Budget at Completion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** The above formulas are part of **Earned Value Management (EVM)** which is covered in detail in Module 3 but is **integrally tested** with Module 4 closure and oversight questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real engineering practice, project closure and oversight ensure:
- **Civil Engineering:** A bridge project is formally handed over to PWD (Public Works Department, Kerala) with as-built drawings, load-test certificates, and warranty documents.
- **Software Engineering:** A deployed ERP system is closed after UAT (User Acceptance Testing) sign-off, with the source code handed to the maintenance team.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** A custom manufacturing line is closed after FAT (Factory Acceptance Test) and SAT (Site Acceptance Test) with full O&M manuals.
- **Government Projects (KTU-relevant):** Compliance audits by the **AG (Auditor General) Office** and **C&AG (Comptroller and Auditor General)** require rigorous closure documentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Closure Decision Matrix

### 3.1 The Step-by-Step Project Closure Process (Detailed Walkthrough)

Below is the **KTU-mandated 10-step closure procedure** with the underlying logic for each step.

**Step 1: Verify All Deliverables Are Complete**
- Logic: Closure cannot begin with open deliverables. Use the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** as the checklist.
- Output: A **Deliverable Completion Matrix** showing 100% completion of every WBS element.

**Step 2: Conduct Final Quality Inspection**
- Logic: Quality is verified *before* acceptance, not after. Use the **Quality Management Plan**.
- Output: A signed **Quality Inspection Report** and a **Punch List** (minor items to fix).

**Step 3: Obtain Formal Acceptance from the Sponsor/Customer**
- Logic: Acceptance transfers ownership and risk. Without it, the project is technically still in execution.
- Output: **Signed Acceptance Certificate** from the sponsor.

**Step 4: Complete All Contractual Obligations**
- Logic: Open contracts = open legal and financial liability.
- Output: **Contract Closure Certificates** from all vendors and subcontractors.

**Step 5: Perform Final Financial Reconciliation**
- Logic: All money must be accounted for to pass the audit.
- Output: **Final Financial Statement** with variance analysis.

**Step 6: Document and Archive Project Records**
- Logic: Archiving enables future projects to benefit from historical data.
- Output: **Project Archive Index** filed in the PMO repository.

**Step 7: Conduct the Lessons Learned Workshop**
- Logic: Captures tacit knowledge that would otherwise be lost when the team disbands.
- Output: A finalized **Lessons Learned Register** and **Retrospective Report**.

**Step 8: Release Project Resources**
- Logic: Idle resources cost money. Formal release is required for HR and asset management.
- Output: **Resource Release Memorandum** signed by functional managers.

**Step 9: Communicate Closure to All Stakeholders**
- Logic: Closure must be communicated to manage stakeholder expectations and reputation.
- Output: **Project Closure Communication** to all internal and external stakeholders.

**Step 10: Conduct the Post-Implementation Review (PIR)**
- Logic: Often done 3–6 months *after* closure to verify benefits realization.
- Output: **PIR Report** comparing planned benefits vs. actual benefits.

### 3.2 The Mathematical Closure Decision Matrix

A common KTU Part A question asks: *Given a project's EVM metrics, determine whether the project is ready for closure or requires corrective action.* The following worked analytical framework demonstrates this:

> **Worked Analytical Example — KTU 2024 Exam Style (3-mark logic)**

**Given Data:**
- $BAC = \text{Rs. } 50{,}00{,}000$ (Budget at Completion)
- $EV = \text{Rs. } 45{,}00{,}000$ (Earned Value)
- $AC = \text{Rs. } 48{,}00{,}000$ (Actual Cost)
- $PV = \text{Rs. } 47{,}00{,}000$ (Planned Value)

**Step 1 — Compute Cost Variance (CV):**

$$
CV = EV - AC
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
CV = 45{,}00{,}000 - 48{,}00{,}000 = -3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret CV:** Since $CV \lt 0$, the project is **over budget** by Rs. 3,00,000. Closure decision: **HOLD closure; trigger corrective action.**

**Step 3 — Compute Cost Performance Index (CPI):**

$$
CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
CPI = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{48{,}00{,}000} = 0.9375
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret CPI:** Since $CPI \lt 1$, the project is inefficient. **Closure is not recommended** without a recovery plan.

**Step 5 — Compute Estimate at Completion (EAC):**

$$
EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
EAC = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.9375} \approx 53{,}33{,}333
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Variance at Completion (VAC):**

$$
VAC = BAC - EAC
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
VAC = 50{,}00{,}000 - 53{,}33{,}333 = -3{,}33{,}333
$$

**Step 7 — Final Decision:** The project will likely **overshoot the budget by Rs. 3,33,333**. The project oversight committee should **defer closure** and initiate a **Change Control Board (CCB)** review.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Logic:** A negative $CV$, $CPI \lt 1$, and negative $VAC$ collectively indicate that the project is financially unhealthy. The **oversight gate** must not be passed under these conditions.

### 3.3 The Project Oversight Maturity Model (POMM) — Analytical Levels

Oversight maturity can be classified into five levels. This is a high-yield theoretical framework for KTU 14-mark questions.

| Level | Oversight Characteristic | Organizational Behavior | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Initial** | No oversight; reactive firefighting | Ad-hoc project audits | "Ad hoc" or "firefighting" |
| **Level 2 — Repeated** | Basic oversight after major issues | Lessons captured informally | "Basic" or "Informal" |
| **Level 3 — Defined** | Structured oversight with documented processes | PMO established with standard gates | "Documented" or "Stage-Gate" |
| **Level 4 — Managed** | Quantitative oversight using EVM and KPIs | Data-driven steering committee reviews | "Quantitative" or "EVM-based" |
| **Level 5 — Optimizing** | Continuous improvement and predictive oversight | AI-driven risk sensing, real-time dashboards | "Predictive" or "Continuous" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Project Closure Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Project Execution Phase Complete]) --> stepB[Step 1: Verify All Deliverables]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Final Quality Inspection]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Punch List Generation]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Sponsor Acceptance Sign-off]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Contract Closure with Vendors]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Financial Reconciliation and Audit]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Document and Archive Records]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Conduct Lessons Learned Workshop]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Release Project Resources]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Communicate Closure to Stakeholders]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 11: Post Implementation Review after 3-6 months]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 12: Final Project Closure Report Archived]
    stepM --> endN([Project Formally Closed])

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style endN fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepB fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepC fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepD fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepF fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepG fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepH fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepI fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepJ fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepK fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepL fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepM fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Explanation of Diagram:** The flowchart visualizes the linear, sequential nature of project closure. Note that **Step 4 (Sponsor Acceptance)** acts as the *critical gate* — without it, the closure process cannot legally proceed. Steps 5–7 (orange) are finance and contract-oriented. Steps 8–10 (pink) are knowledge and resource-oriented. Steps 11–12 (purple/green) represent the *post-closure* phase, which many students mistakenly omit.

### 4.2 Three-Layer Project Oversight Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1 [LAYER 1 INTERNAL OVERSIGHT]
        dir1[Sponsor] --> dir2[Steering Committee]
        dir2 --> dir3[Stage Gate Reviews]
    end

    subgraph layer2 [LAYER 2 ORGANIZATIONAL OVERSIGHT]
        dir4[PMO] --> dir5[Portfolio Dashboard]
        dir5 --> dir6[Compliance Audit]
    end

    subgraph layer3 [LAYER 3 INDEPENDENT OVERSIGHT]
        dir7[External Auditors] --> dir8[Regulatory Bodies]
        dir8 --> dir9[Public Accountability Review]
    end

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> feedback1[Feedback to Project Manager]
    feedback1 --> pm[Project Manager]

    style dir1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir2 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir3 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir4 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir5 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir6 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir7 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir8 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dir9 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pm fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style feedback1 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Explanation of Diagram:** This architecture shows the *hierarchical* nature of project oversight. The three layers operate concurrently and feed back corrective signals to the project manager. The **arrows from Layer 3 to feedback to PM** illustrate the *closed-loop governance* principle — oversight is not a one-way audit, but a feedback system.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Project Audit Trail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs: Project Records] --> B[Document Verification Module]
    B --> C[Compliance Check Module]
    C --> D[Variance Analysis Module]
    D --> E[Risk Re-Assessment Module]
    E --> F[Audit Report Generation]
    F --> G[Closure Recommendation]
    G --> H{Approval Decision}
    H --> I[Approved: Close Project]
    H --> J[Rejected: Initiate Recovery]

    style A fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Explanation of Diagram:** This is the **block-level functional architecture** of the project audit trail. Each block represents a functional stage. The diamond-shaped decision block (H) determines whether the project is approved for closure or sent back for recovery. This is a frequently tested architecture in KTU 14-mark questions on oversight.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** Define **Project Closure**. List any **four key activities** performed during the project closure phase.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Project Closure is the formal and structured process of finalizing all project activities, formally accepting the project deliverables from the sponsor, completing all contracts, releasing project resources, and archiving all project documentation to officially terminate the project.

**Four Key Activities (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Final Deliverable Acceptance** — Obtaining formal sign-off from the sponsor/customer.
2. **Contract Closure** — Settling and closing all vendor and supplier contracts.
3. **Lessons Learned Documentation** — Conducting a retrospective and capturing insights in the Lessons Learned Register.
4. **Resource Release and Archival** — Releasing team members and archiving all project records in the PMO repository.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** Students often lose 0.5 marks by writing the *definition* without enumerating the activities. Always pair a definition with structured points.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** Differentiate between **Project Oversight** and **Project Management**. Give one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Project Management | Project Oversight |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To *execute* the project and deliver the outputs | To *govern* and *assure* that the project is executed correctly |
| **Performed by** | The **Project Manager** | The **Sponsor, Steering Committee, PMO, and Auditors** |
| **Time horizon** | Day-to-day, throughout the project lifecycle | Periodic — at stage-gates or audit milestones |
| **Tool used** | Schedule, budget, scope management | EVM reports, dashboards, compliance audits |

**Example 1 (1 mark):** Project Management — *A project manager tracking the daily progress of a bridge construction site in Kerala.*

**Example 2 (1 mark):** Project Oversight — *The Kerala PWD Chief Engineer reviewing the monthly EVM dashboard and conducting a stage-gate audit at 50% completion.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Cue:** Use the phrase *"Doing the project vs. Ensuring the right project is being done right"* to score full conceptual marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Students must answer either OR choice. Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model | Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **six pillars of project closure** in detail. Why is each pillar important? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A project has the following data at the end of Month 8:
- $BAC = \text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $EV = \text{Rs. } 80{,}00{,}000$
- $AC = \text{Rs. } 90{,}00{,}000$
- $PV = \text{Rs. } 85{,}00{,}000$

Compute the **CPI, SPI, EAC, and VAC**. Based on these metrics, **decide whether the project should be closed** or sent for corrective action. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Six Pillars of Project Closure (7 marks):**

*(Examiner breakdown: 1 mark per pillar + 0.16 for the "why" of each pillar; 1 mark for the introduction/importance summary)*

**1. Final Deliverable Acceptance (1.16 marks):**
- The sponsor/customer formally verifies that all deliverables match the agreed scope and quality.
- **Why:** Without formal acceptance, the project cannot legally close. It also triggers the warranty period.

**2. Contract Closure (Procurement Closure) (1.16 marks):**
- All vendor and supplier contracts are formally settled, final payments released, and warranty documents exchanged.
- **Why:** Open contracts create ongoing legal liability and financial exposure.

**3. Financial Closure (1.16 marks):**
- Reconciliation of all financial accounts, payment of all pending dues, and final cost audit.
- **Why:** Required for statutory compliance and for accurate historical cost data.

**4. Administrative Closure (1.16 marks):**
- Updating and archiving all project management plans, baselines, and records.
- **Why:** Enables organizational learning and supports future project estimates.

**5. Lessons Learned and Knowledge Transfer (1.16 marks):**
- A structured retrospective is conducted. The Lessons Learned Register is finalized and transferred to the PMO.
- **Why:** Prevents the repetition of mistakes and builds institutional memory.

**6. Resource Release and Team Transition (1.16 marks):**
- Team members are formally released to functional managers; project-specific assets are reassigned.
- **Why:** Idle resources are a cost; formal release enables reassignment and HR closure.

**Summary Statement (0.04 marks for conclusion):**
The six pillars ensure that the project ends with **legal, financial, administrative, and intellectual completeness** — not just completion of the technical work.

---

**Part (b) — EVM Computation and Closure Decision (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Cost Performance Index (CPI):**

$$
CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
CPI = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{90{,}00{,}000} = 0.8889
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Schedule Performance Index (SPI):**

$$
SPI = \frac{EV}{PV}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
SPI = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{85{,}00{,}000} = 0.9412
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Estimate at Completion (EAC):**

$$
EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
EAC = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{0.8889} \approx 1{,}12{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Variance at Completion (VAC):**

$$
VAC = BAC - EAC
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
VAC = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}12{,}50{,}000 = -12{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cost Variance (CV) and Schedule Variance (SV):**

$$
CV = EV - AC = 80{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}00{,}000 = -10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
SV = EV - PV = 80{,}00{,}000 - 85{,}00{,}000 = -5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Results:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $CV$ | $-10{,}00{,}000$ | Over budget |
| $SV$ | $-5{,}00{,}000$ | Behind schedule |
| $CPI$ | $0.8889 \lt 1$ | Inefficient cost performance |
| $SPI$ | $0.9412 \lt 1$ | Behind schedule |
| $EAC$ | $1{,}12{,}50{,}000$ | Final cost will exceed budget |
| $VAC$ | $-12{,}50{,}000$ | Anticipated overrun of Rs. 12,50,000 |

**Step 7 — Final Decision:**
The project is **NOT READY for closure**. The negative $CV$, $SV$, and $VAC$ indicate the project is **over budget and behind schedule**. The project should be **referred to the Change Control Board (CCB)** for corrective action, including scope re-baselining, crash planning, or schedule recovery, before the closure gate is passed.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model | Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **three layers of project oversight** in detail. How does each layer contribute to project governance? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the **Project Audit** process. What are the **steps**, **types**, and **outputs** of a project audit? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Three Layers of Project Oversight (7 marks):**

**Layer 1 — Internal Oversight (2.5 marks):**
- **Performed by:** Project Sponsor and Steering Committee.
- **Frequency:** Monthly, or at every major milestone.
- **Tools:** Progress reports, EVM dashboards, Risk Registers, Issue Logs.
- **Contribution:** Provides *real-time* governance, ensures alignment with business case, and authorizes major changes.
- **Example:** A monthly steering committee meeting reviewing the status of a Kerala road construction project funded by KIIFB (Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board).

**Layer 2 — Organizational / PMO Oversight (2.5 marks):**
- **Performed by:** The Project Management Office (PMO).
- **Function:** Ensures the project follows organizational methodologies, templates, and standards.
- **Tools:** Portfolio dashboards, methodology compliance checks, gate-review checklists.
- **Contribution:** Provides *standardization* and *strategic alignment* across the project portfolio.
- **Example:** A central PMO in a KTU-affiliated engineering college ensuring that all student capstone projects follow the institute's approved methodology.

**Layer 3 — Independent / External Oversight (2 marks):**
- **Performed by:** External auditors, regulatory bodies, government agencies, public accountability forums.
- **Function:** Provides *independent* assurance that the project is compliant with laws, contracts, and public interest.
- **Tools:** Statutory audits, RTI (Right to Information) reviews, AG audits, parliamentary committees (for public projects).
- **Contribution:** Provides *legitimacy* and *public trust*, especially in government-funded projects.

---

**Part (b) — Project Audit (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Project Audit is a structured, independent review of a project's performance, processes, and compliance with the project management plan and applicable standards.

**Types of Project Audit (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Pre-Audit** — Conducted *before* the project begins to validate the project plan and feasibility.
2. **Mid-Term Audit** — Conducted *during* execution to identify deviations and recommend corrective actions.
3. **Post-Audit** — Conducted *at* or *after* project closure to evaluate overall success and capture lessons.
4. **Compliance Audit** — Focuses on adherence to legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements.

**Steps of a Project Audit (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Define Audit Scope and Objectives** — Establish what will be audited and why.
2. **Gather Project Records** — Collect plans, reports, contracts, financial statements.
3. **Conduct On-Site / Document Review** — Interview stakeholders, inspect deliverables, verify records.
4. **Analyze Performance Against Baselines** — Compare planned vs. actual cost, schedule, scope, and quality.
5. **Identify Findings and Non-Conformities** — Document gaps, deviations, and risks.
6. **Prepare the Audit Report** — Summarize findings, recommendations, and corrective actions.
7. **Present Findings and Follow Up** — Share with the steering committee and track closure of recommendations.

**Outputs of a Project Audit (1 mark):**
- **Audit Report** with findings and recommendations.
- **Corrective Action Plan (CAP)**.
- **Updated Risk Register**.
- **Lessons Learned Contribution**.
- **Updated Project Baselines** (if scope is re-baselined).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Project Closure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "Contract Closure" step** — Many students list only technical closure. Always mention both *administrative* and *contract* closure separately.
> 2. **Confusing "Project Oversight" with "Project Monitoring"** — Oversight is *governance-level* and *independent*; monitoring is *operational-level* by the project manager. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Skipping the "Lessons Learned" step** — This is worth 1.5 marks and is the most overlooked step.
> 4. **Computational errors in EVM formulas** — Mixing up $EV$, $PV$, $AC$, and $BAC$. Memorize the triangle: $CV = EV - AC$ and $SV = EV - PV$.
> 5. **Not stating units in EVM answers** — Always write "Rs. 1,00,00,000" not just "1,00,00,000." Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **Writing generic answers without examples** — Use Kerala-specific examples (KIIFB, PWD, KTU, KMRL, C-DIT) to score higher in KTU 2024 valuations.
> 7. **Ignoring the "Post-Implementation Review"** — Many students end at sign-off. PIR is a *post-closure* activity tested in 14-mark answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4: Project Closure and Oversight**

- **Project Closure** is the *last* of the five PMBOK process groups — it is *mandatory*, not optional.
- The **six pillars** are: Acceptance, Contract Closure, Financial Closure, Administrative Closure, Lessons Learned, Resource Release.
- **Project Oversight** is *independent governance* — it is *not* the same as project management.
- The **three layers** of oversight are: Internal (Sponsor/SC), Organizational (PMO), Independent (External Auditors).
- **Project Audit** is a structured review — remember its *4 types*: Pre, Mid-Term, Post, Compliance.
- The **EVM Closure Metrics** to memorize are: $CV$, $SV$, $CPI$, $SPI$, $EAC$, $VAC$, $TCPI$.
- The **decision rule** for closure: $CPI \ge 1$, $SPI \ge 1$, $VAC \ge 0$ → *Green-light closure*. Otherwise, trigger a Change Control Board review.
- The **Lessons Learned Register** is the most important *intellectual deliverable* of closure.
- **Post-Implementation Review (PIR)** is conducted 3–6 months *after* closure to verify benefit realization.
- **Archival** is non-negotiable — without it, the project record is legally and administratively incomplete.
- A **Punch List** must be closed *before* formal acceptance is granted.
- The **Steering Committee** is the highest oversight body *within* the project; the **PMO** is the highest *organizational* oversight body.
- For **KTU 2024**, expect a mix of (a) theoretical 7-mark questions on closure pillars, (b) numerical 7-mark EVM-based closure decisions, and (c) descriptive 14-mark essays combining closure + audit + oversight layers.
- **Always** structure your 14-mark answer as: *Definition (1 mark) → Enumeration (6 marks) → Diagram (2 marks) → Example (2 marks) → Conclusion (1 mark) → KTU/Real-world reference (2 marks)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Various Project Management Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UEHUT704 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT : PLANNING, EXECUTION,EVALUATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Project Management Software ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Project Management Software (PMS)** refers to a specialized category of digital tools, platforms, and integrated computer applications designed to assist project managers and teams in **planning, organizing, scheduling, tracking, collaborating, and controlling** the resources, tasks, costs, timelines, and deliverables of a project across its entire lifecycle — from initiation to closure. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, Project Management Software acts as a **centralized digital nervous system** that converts strategic project objectives (defined in the Project Management Plan) into actionable, time-bound, resource-allocated, and trackable work items. It enforces the **5 Process Groups** (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing) and the **10 Knowledge Areas** of the PMBOK framework through digital automation. ### 1.2 Categories of Project Management Software | Category | Description | Example Tools | |---|---|---| | **Desktop-based** | Installed locally on a single machine; single-user | Microsoft Project (Standalone) | | **Client-Server / Enterprise** | Installed on organization servers; multi-user | Primavera P6, Deltek Open Plan | | **Cloud-based / SaaS** | Hosted on vendor cloud; subscription model | Asana, Monday.com, Smartsheet, Wrike | | **Open-Source** | Free, customizable, community-supported | OpenProject, Redmine, ProjectLibre | | **Collaborative / Work Management** | Task-oriented, team-first design | Trello, ClickUp, Notion | | **Agile-Specific** | Built for Scrum, Kanban, sprints | Jira, Azure DevOps, Targetprocess | | **Construction/Engineering-specific** | Heavy focus on scheduling, BIM, cost | Primavera P6, Asta Powerproject, Planner | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Project Manager's "Smart Kitchen"** > Imagine you are running a large restaurant kitchen serving 200 dishes simultaneously. > - **The Recipe Book** = Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) > - **The Order Tickets** = Tasks with deadlines > - **The Cooking Stations** = Resources (chefs, ovens, prep tables) > - **The Inventory Register** = Cost & material tracking > - **The Waiter's Headset** = Real-time team communication > > A **Project Management Software** is the *integrated digital kitchen dashboard* that displays — on a single screen — which dish (task) is on which stove (resource), what time it will be ready (schedule), how much fuel is being used (cost), and which waiter (team member) has been alerted. Without it, the head chef (Project Manager) is shouting across a noisy kitchen. With it, everything is visible, traceable, and controllable. ### 1.4 Core Functions of PM Software Every PM software, regardless of brand, must perform these **6 universal functions**: 1. **Task Definition & WBS Creation** — Decompose deliverables into actionable work items. 2. **Scheduling** — Build Gantt charts, assign durations, dependencies (FS, SS, FF, SF). 3. **Resource Allocation & Leveling** — Assign people, equipment, materials; resolve over-allocations. 4. **Cost Management** — Budget tracking, EVM (Earned Value Management), forecasting. 5. **Collaboration & Communication** — File sharing, comments, notifications, dashboards. 6. **Reporting & Analytics** — Status reports, S-curves, burn-down charts, KPI dashboards. ### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in PM Software > [!IMPORTANT] > - **CPI (Cost Performance Index)** — bold target value = **1.0** (on budget) > - **SPI (Schedule Performance Index)** — bold target value = **1.0** (on schedule) > - **EAC (Estimate at Completion)** — Forecasted total project cost > - **BAC (Budget at Completion)** — Approved total project budget > - **Resource Utilization %** — bold healthy range = **70% – 85%** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why PM Software Matters The **Theory of Project Management Automation** rests on three pillars: - **Pillar 1 — Centralization of Information (Single Source of Truth)** Eliminates version-control chaos. Every stakeholder accesses the *same* baseline, the *same* Gantt, the *same* cost sheet. - **Pillar 2 — Algorithmic Computation (Critical Path & EVM)** Modern software implements algorithms (CPM, PERT, Monte Carlo) that manually would take days. Critical Path is auto-recalculated upon any change. - **Pillar 3 — Real-Time Visibility & Predictive Analytics** Dashboards transform raw data into actionable signals. AI-driven tools now predict slippage before it happens. ### 2.2 Selection Criteria for PM Software (KTU Exam Favorite) A KTU examiner typically asks: *"Discuss the criteria for selecting project management software."* The standard answer framework is: | # | Criterion | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Project Size & Complexity** | Mega-construction projects need Primavera; small teams may use Trello | | 2 | **Cost (Licensing & TCO)** | One-time purchase vs SaaS subscription; training cost | | 3 | **Scalability** | Can it grow with multiple concurrent projects? | | 4 | **Integration Capability** | Compatibility with ERP (SAP), MS Office, AutoCAD, BIM | | 5 | **User-Friendliness & Learning Curve** | Stakeholder adoption rate | | 6 | **Security & Data Ownership** | On-premise gives full control; cloud gives vendor-managed | | 7 | **Collaboration Features** | Real-time editing, mobile access, chat | | 8 | **Reporting & Analytics** | Customizable dashboards, export to PDF/Excel | | 9 | **Industry Specificity** | Construction vs IT vs Manufacturing | | 10 | **Vendor Support & Training** | 24/7 support, certification programs | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (EVM Computed in Software) The following formulas are auto-computed by most PM software and form a **high-weightage cluster** in KTU numerical questions: | Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Healthy Value | |---|---|---|---| | $PV$ | $\text{Planned Value (BCWS)}$ | Budgeted cost of work scheduled | Baseline | | $EV$ | $\text{Earned Value (BCWP)}$ | Budgeted cost of work performed | Progress metric | | $AC$ | $\text{Actual Cost (ACWP)}$ | Real cost incurred so far | Actual | | $CV$ | $EV - AC$ | Cost Variance | $> 0$ is good | | $SV$ | $EV - PV$ | Schedule Variance | $> 0$ is good | | $CPI$ | $\dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | Cost Performance Index | $\geq 1.0$ | | $SPI$ | $\dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | Schedule Performance Index | $\geq 1.0$ | | $EAC$ | $\dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Estimate at Completion | Should be $\leq BAC$ | | $ETC$ | $EAC - AC$ | Estimate to Complete | Remaining budget | | $VAC$ | $BAC - EAC$ | Variance at Completion | $\geq 0$ is good | | $TCPI$ | $\dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | To-Complete Performance Index | $\leq 1.0$ achievable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always state units. Cost is in monetary units (₹, $, €), time in days/weeks/months, and indices are dimensionless ratios. ### 2.4 Comparison of Top PM Software (KTU Reference Table) | Feature | **MS Project** | **Primavera P6** | **Smartsheet** | **Jira** | **Asana** | **Trello** | **Monday.com** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Desktop/Cloud | Enterprise (Client-Server) | Cloud SaaS | Cloud (Agile) | Cloud SaaS | Cloud (Kanban) | Cloud SaaS | | **Best For** | General PM | Mega Construction/Engineering | Mid-size teams | IT/Software (Scrum) | Marketing/Creative | Small teams/Startups | Cross-functional | | **Gantt Chart** | ✅ Advanced | ✅ Advanced | ✅ Basic | ❌ (use plugins) | ✅ Basic | ❌ (use plugins) | ✅ Basic | | **Critical Path Method** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Resource Leveling** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Limited | ❌ No | ✅ Limited | ❌ No | ✅ Basic | | **Cost & EVM** | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Basic | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Basic | | **Multi-Project Mgmt** | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Excellent (Unlimited) | ✅ Good | ✅ Good | ✅ Good | ❌ Single board | ✅ Good | | **Collaboration** | ⚠ Basic | ⚠ Basic | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | | **Mobile App** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Learning Curve** | Moderate | Steep | Easy | Moderate | Easy | Very Easy | Easy | | **Price Tier** | Mid | High | Mid | Mid (free tier) | Mid (free tier) | Low (free tier) | Mid | | **Integration** | MS 365, Teams | ERP, Oracle, BIM | MS 365, Slack, GSuite | GitHub, Bitbucket, CI/CD | Slack, Zoom, GSuite | Slack, GSuite, Zapier | 200+ apps | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: How PM Software Computes the Critical Path The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is the foundational scheduling algorithm used by every enterprise PM software (MS Project, Primavera P6, etc.). The derivation below shows how the software arrives at project duration mathematically. #### Step 1 — Define the Network Let a project have $n$ activities. Each activity $i$ has: - Duration $D_i$ - Predecessors $P_i$ (set of activities that must finish before $i$ starts) - Earliest Start $ES_i$ and Earliest Finish $EF_i$ #### Step 2 — Forward Pass (Left to Right) The software computes Earliest Start and Earliest Finish for every node: $$ ES_i = \max_{j \in P_i} \left( EF_j \right) $$ $$ EF_i = ES_i + D_i $$ **Explanation:** The earliest $i$ can start is when the *latest* of its predecessors has finished. Then it runs for its own duration. #### Step 3 — Backward Pass (Right to Left) The software computes Latest Start and Latest Finish from the project end: $$ LF_i = \min_{k \in S_i} \left( LS_k \right) $$ $$ LS_i = LF_i - D_i $$ where $S_i$ = set of successors of activity $i$. **Explanation:** Working backward from the project deadline, we find the *latest* an activity can finish without delaying the project. #### Step 4 — Compute Total Float $$ TF_i = LS_i - ES_i \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad TF_i = LF_i - EF_i $$ **Explanation:** Total Float is the "slack" — the time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date. #### Step 5 — Identify the Critical Path $$ \text{Activity } i \text{ is CRITICAL} \iff TF_i = 0 $$ The chain of zero-float activities from project start to project end forms the **Critical Path**, and the sum of their durations equals the **Minimum Project Duration** $T_{min}$: $$ T_{min} = \sum_{i \in \text{Critical Path}} D_i $$ > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example — Small Network** > > Consider four activities: A (3 days) → B (4 days) → D (2 days), and A → C (5 days) → D. > > Step 1: $ES_A = 0$, $EF_A = 3$. > Step 2: $ES_B = EF_A = 3$, $EF_B = 7$. $ES_C = EF_A = 3$, $EF_C = 8$. > Step 3: $ES_D = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(7, 8) = 8$, $EF_D = 10$. > > Therefore $T_{min} = 10$ days, and the critical path is **A → C → D** (duration 3+5+2 = 10). Activity B has $TF_B = 8 - 7 = 1$ day of slack. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: EVM Computation Inside Software Given: $BAC = 10{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $PV = 4{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $EV = 3{,}60{,}000$ ₹, $AC = 4{,}50{,}000$ ₹ at 40% scheduled completion. **Step-by-step solution showing what the software does internally:** $$ CV = EV - AC = 3{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = -90{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Interpretation: The project is **over budget** by ₹90,000.* $$ SV = EV - PV = 3{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = -40{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Interpretation: The project is **behind schedule** by ₹40,000 worth of work.* $$ CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{3{,}60{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = 0.80 $$ > *Interpretation: For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of work is being earned.* $$ SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{3{,}60{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.90 $$ > *Interpretation: Only 90% of the scheduled work has been completed.* $$ EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = 12{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Interpretation: If current performance continues, the project will cost ₹12.5 Lakhs — **25% over budget**.* $$ VAC = BAC - EAC = 10{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}50{,}000 = -2{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Interpretation: There will be a final cost overrun of ₹2.5 Lakhs.* $$ TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000} = \dfrac{6{,}40{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} = 1.1636 $$ > *Interpretation: The remaining work must be done at ₹1.16 worth of productivity per ₹1 spent to recover.* ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Software | Scenario | Recommended Software | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Building a 50-floor skyscraper | **Primavera P6** | Multi-project, multi-resource, BIM integration, EAC forecasting | | Small IT startup building a mobile app | **Trello or Jira** | Agile sprints, Kanban boards, fast iteration | | Marketing agency handling 20 campaigns | **Asana or Monday.com** | Visual workflows, collaboration, client portals | | University thesis with 5 milestones | **MS Project (Student)** | Free with student license; teaches CPM/EVM formally | | Distributed team across 3 continents | **ClickUp or Smartsheet** | Cloud-based, time-zone aware, real-time sync | | Government project with strict audit trail | **Primavera P6 / Deltek** | Compliance, document control, role-based access | | Personal / hobby project | **Trello (Free)** | Free, simple, Kanban-style | | Software development with CI/CD | **Jira + Confluence** | Issue tracking, sprint planning, code integration | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Project Management Software. List any **four** categories of project management software. **Model Answer:** > **Project Management Software (PMS)** is a digital tool used by project managers and teams to plan, schedule, track, allocate resources, control costs, and report on project performance across the project lifecycle. > > **Four Categories:** > 1. **Desktop-based Software** (e.g., standalone MS Project) > 2. **Enterprise / Client-Server Software** (e.g., Primavera P6, Deltek) > 3. **Cloud-based / SaaS Software** (e.g., Asana, Monday.com, Smartsheet) > 4. **Open-Source Software** (e.g., OpenProject, Redmine, ProjectLibre) > > *(Other acceptable: Agile-specific, Collaborative/Work Management, Industry-specific)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Microsoft Project** and **Primavera P6** based on their intended use and key features. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Microsoft Project** | **Primavera P6** | |---|---|---| | **Intended Use** | Small to medium single projects | Mega, multi-project, engineering/construction | | **Critical Path Method** | Yes | Yes (advanced) | | **Resource Leveling** | Basic | Advanced with role-based optimization | | **Multi-Project Handling** | Limited (master projects) | Excellent (EPS structure) | | **EVM Support** | Yes | Yes (more granular) | | **Learning Curve** | Moderate | Steep | | **Cost** | Lower (perpetual license) | Higher (enterprise license) | | **Best For** | General PM, training, mid-size | Construction, oil & gas, EPC, government | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **criteria for selecting project management software** for an organization. Illustrate with an example of a real-world construction project. **(b) [7 Marks)** Compare and contrast **Cloud-based (SaaS) and On-Premise (Enterprise)** project management software in terms of cost, security, scalability, accessibility, and maintenance. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Selecting PM Software — 10 Key Criteria:** 1. **Project Size & Complexity** — A 50-floor tower project with 5,000 activities needs Primavera, not Trello. 2. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — Includes licensing, training, hardware (for on-premise), upgrades. 3. **Scalability** — Must support growth from 1 to 100 concurrent projects. 4. **Integration Capability** — Must connect with ERP, BIM (Revit), MS Office, AutoCAD. 5. **User-Friendliness** — Low adoption = low ROI on software investment. 6. **Security & Compliance** — ISO 27001, GDPR, government audit trails. 7. **Collaboration Features** — Real-time editing, mobile access for site engineers. 8. **Reporting & Analytics** — Customizable dashboards for sponsors. 9. **Industry Specificity** — Construction needs CPM, IT needs Agile. 10. **Vendor Support & Training** — 24/7 support, certified trainers, community forums. > **Real-World Example — Delhi Metro Phase-4 Construction:** > The Delhi Metro Rail Corporation selected **Primavera P6** because: (i) it handles 100+ concurrent work packages, (ii) integrates with their SAP ERP for vendor payment, (iii) supports Earned Value Analysis for government reporting, (iv) provides role-based access for 5,000+ workers, and (v) generates compliance-ready reports for the Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Cloud SaaS vs On-Premise PM Software:** | Parameter | **Cloud SaaS (Asana, Monday)** | **On-Premise (Primavera P6, Deltek)** | |---|---|---| | **Cost** | Subscription (monthly/annual); low upfront | High upfront licensing + hardware; long-term ROI | | **Security** | Vendor-managed; trust in cloud provider | Full organizational control; data stays in-house | | **Scalability** | Elastic — scale users/projects instantly | Limited by server capacity; manual upgrade | | **Accessibility** | Anywhere with internet; mobile-first | Limited to VPN/intranet; site access difficult | | **Maintenance** | Vendor handles updates, backups, uptime | Internal IT team manages updates, patches | | **Customization** | Limited to vendor's roadmap | Fully customizable workflows | | **Disaster Recovery** | Vendor's geo-redundant data centers | Organization must build & test DR | | **Time to Deploy** | Days to weeks | Months (procurement, install, configure) | > **Conclusion:** Cloud SaaS suits *agile, distributed, cost-sensitive* teams. On-Premise suits *mega, regulated, security-critical* projects. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six core functions** that any project management software must perform. How do these map to the PMBOK 5 Process Groups? **(b) [7 Marks]** A construction project has the following data at the 40% scheduled milestone: $BAC = 50{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $PV = 20{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $EV = 18{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $AC = 22{,}50{,}000$ ₹. Compute and interpret **CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC**, and **TCPI**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Six Core Functions of PM Software + PMBOK Mapping:** | # | Core Function | Description | PMBOK Process Group | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Task Definition & WBS Creation** | Decompose scope into work packages | Planning | | 2 | **Scheduling (CPM/PERT)** | Build Gantt, sequence activities, identify critical path | Planning | | 3 | **Resource Allocation & Leveling** | Assign people, equipment, balance workload | Planning / Executing | | 4 | **Cost & Budget Management** | Track costs, compute EVM indicators | Planning / Monitoring & Controlling | | 5 | **Collaboration & Communication** | File sharing, chat, comments, notifications | Executing | | 6 | **Reporting & Analytics** | Dashboards, status reports, KPI tracking | Monitoring & Controlling | > **Conclusion:** A robust PM software should cover *all 5 PMBOK Process Groups* — Initiating (charter), Planning (WBS/schedule/budget), Executing (collaboration), Monitoring & Controlling (EVM, dashboards), and Closing (lessons learned archive). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical **Given:** $BAC = 50{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $PV = 20{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $EV = 18{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $AC = 22{,}50{,}000$ ₹. **Step 1 — Cost Variance:** $$ CV = EV - AC = 18{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}50{,}000 = -4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Project is **over budget** by ₹4,50,000.* **Step 2 — Schedule Variance:** $$ SV = EV - PV = 18{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Project is **behind schedule** (worth ₹2,00,000 of work not done).* **Step 3 — Cost Performance Index:** $$ CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{22{,}50{,}000} = 0.80 $$ > *For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of work is being earned. **Unfavourable.*** **Step 4 — Schedule Performance Index:** $$ SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.90 $$ > *Only 90% of scheduled work is complete. **Behind schedule.*** **Step 5 — Estimate at Completion:** $$ EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = 62{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Final cost projected at ₹62.5 Lakhs — **25% over budget.*** **Step 6 — Variance at Completion:** $$ VAC = BAC - EAC = 50{,}00{,}000 - 62{,}50{,}000 = -12{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ > *Final cost overrun of **₹12,50,000** expected.* **Step 7 — To-Complete Performance Index:** $$ TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}50{,}000} = \dfrac{32{,}00{,}000}{27{,}50{,}000} = 1.1636 $$ > *Remaining work must be done at ₹1.16 productivity per ₹1 spent — **very challenging.*** > **Examiner's Conclusion:** The project is in **double trouble** — over budget AND behind schedule. Corrective action: scope reduction, crash the schedule, or accept the variance formally. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing CV with Cost Index** — $CV$ is in ₹, $CPI$ is a dimensionless ratio. Never mix them. > 2. **Forgetting the Units** — Always write ₹, days, etc. A number without a unit gets partial credit only. > 3. **Sign Error in SV** — $SV = EV - PV$, NOT $PV - EV$. A positive SV is *good* (ahead of schedule). > 4. **Skipping Interpretation** — KTU award marks for *interpretation* ("Over budget", "Unfavourable"), not just the calculation. Always end with a one-line business interpretation. > 5. **Wrong EAC Formula** — Use $EAC = BAC / CPI$ **only when current performance is representative**. For a different formula (e.g., $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$), the problem must explicitly state it. > 6. **Listing PM software without categories** — The question says "various software" — always group them (Desktop, Cloud, Enterprise, Open-Source) to score full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Introduction to PM Software** > > ✅ **Definition** — PMS = digital tool for planning, scheduling, tracking, costing, collaborating, reporting. > > ✅ **Seven Categories** — Desktop | Enterprise | Cloud SaaS | Open-Source | Agile | Collaborative | Industry-Specific. > > ✅ **Six Universal Functions** — WBS | Scheduling | Resources | Cost/EVM | Collaboration | Reporting. > > ✅ **Critical Path = Zero Float** — CPM auto-computed in MS Project & Primavera P6. > > ✅ **EVM Healthy Values** — $CPI \geq 1.0$, $SPI \geq 1.0$, $VAC \geq 0$, $TCPI \leq 1.0$. > > ✅ **Top 7 Tools to Remember** — MS Project | Primavera P6 | Smartsheet | Asana | Trello | Monday.com | Jira. > > ✅ **Primavera P6 = Mega Construction / EPC** | **MS Project = General / Training** | **Trello = Startups / Kanban** | **Jira = IT / Scrum**. > > ✅ **Cloud vs On-Premise** — Cloud = cheap, fast, accessible | On-Premise = secure, customizable, expensive. > > ✅ **Selection Criteria Mnemonic — "CISSICaRVS"** — Cost | Integration | Scalability | Security | Industry | Collaboration | Reporting | Vendor | Accessibility | Size. > > ✅ **AI & Future Trend** — Modern PMS uses AI for predictive slippage alerts, auto-scheduling, and resource forecasting. > > ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus** — Expect a 14-mark question blending *software comparison* with an *EVM numerical problem* OR a *selection criteria discussion*.
